description: Marine SES DAPSIWRM knowledge database - context-specific element and connection suggestions for Social-Ecological Systems analysis. Each context represents a regional sea x habitat combination with ecologically plausible elements and scientifically defensible connections.

baltic_lagoon.description: Baltic coastal lagoons (e.g., Curonian, Vistula, Szczecin). Shallow, brackish, highly productive systems with limited water exchange, extensive reed beds, and strong agricultural nutrient inputs.

activities.name: Reed harvesting for thatching and bioenergy
Recreational angling for pike and perch

pressures.name: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
Cyanobacteria bloom formation
Reduced water exchange with open sea
Sedimentation and lagoon infilling
Microplastic accumulation in sediments
Fishing mortality on pike-perch stock

states.name: Round goby and Chinese mitten crab (Neogobius melanostomus, Eriocheir sinensis) invasion
Zostera marina (eelgrass/seagrass) bed extent and condition

impacts.name: Fish provisioning from lagoon fisheries
Water purification by reed beds and macrophytes
Bird habitat provision (breeding, staging)
Flood buffering and coastal protection

welfare.name: Fisher household income from pike-perch sales
Recreational value for local communities
Cultural identity of lagoon fishing villages
Tourism revenue from birdwatching and angling

responses.name: HELCOM nutrient reduction targets for lagoon catchments
Reed bed restoration programmes
Pike-perch minimum landing size regulations
Agricultural buffer zone requirements
Natura 2000 lagoon habitat protection

connections.to: Agricultural drainage into lagoon
connections.rationale: Intensified farming increases fertiliser use and drainage volumes flowing into the lagoon

connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Drainage channels deliver nitrogen and phosphorus directly to the lagoon, driving eutrophication
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Cyanobacteria bloom formation
connections.rationale: Excess nutrients, especially phosphorus, fuel cyanobacteria blooms in warm shallow lagoons. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.to: Submerged macrophyte coverage (e.g., Potamogeton)
connections.rationale: Nutrient loading increases phytoplankton turbidity, shading out submerged macrophytes
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Water transparency and light penetration
connections.rationale: Dense cyanobacteria blooms drastically reduce water clarity in shallow lagoons. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Reduced water exchange with open sea
connections.to: Phytoplankton community composition
connections.rationale: Poor flushing allows nutrient accumulation and dominance by bloom-forming species. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.to: Fish provisioning from lagoon fisheries
connections.rationale: Healthy pike-perch stocks directly support lagoon fishery catches. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Bird habitat provision (breeding, staging)
connections.rationale: Extensive reed beds provide essential nesting and staging habitat for waterbirds

connections.to: Water purification by reed beds and macrophytes
connections.rationale: Reed beds filter nutrients and trap sediment, improving water quality. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Fisher household income from pike-perch sales
connections.rationale: Fish catches directly translate to fisher income in lagoon communities. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Tourism revenue from birdwatching and angling
connections.rationale: Rich bird populations attract birdwatching tourists and generate local revenue. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally

connections.from: HELCOM nutrient reduction targets for lagoon catchments
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: International nutrient targets drive national measures that reduce agricultural nutrient loads

connections.to: Agricultural drainage into lagoon
connections.rationale: Buffer zones intercept nutrients before they reach drainage channels flowing to the lagoon
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Small-scale gillnet fishing for pike-perch
connections.rationale: Minimum size limits reduce fishing pressure on juvenile pike-perch. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.to: Small-scale gillnet fishing for pike-perch
connections.rationale: Local food security needs directly drive small-scale gillnet fishing effort targeting pike-perch in lagoons
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Fisher household income from pike-perch sales
connections.to: Food security from local fisheries
connections.rationale: Fisher income levels influence community investment in and demand for continued local fishery production
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Agricultural drainage into lagoon
connections.rationale: Natura 2000 conservation obligations impose restrictions on agricultural drainage practices affecting lagoon habitats
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Reduced water exchange with open sea
connections.rationale: Progressive sedimentation narrows and shallows lagoon inlets, further restricting water exchange with the open sea and exacerbating stagnation

connections.to: Water transparency and light penetration
connections.rationale: Submerged macrophytes stabilise sediments and reduce resuspension, maintaining clear water; macrophyte loss triggers a turbid-water stable state through positive feedback with phytoplankton dominance

connections.to: Pike-perch population dynamics
connections.rationale: Shift from diatom to cyanobacteria dominance reduces zooplankton food quality, impacting pike-perch larval survival through trophic cascade effects on prey availability
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Fishing mortality on pike-perch stock
connections.rationale: Gillnet fishing directly creates fishing mortality pressure on pike-perch stocks. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Pike-perch population dynamics
connections.rationale: Fishing mortality reduces pike-perch stock size and alters population age structure. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Small-scale gillnet fishing for pike-perch
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Recreational demand for nature tourism
references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Urban wastewater management needs
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Small-scale gillnet fishing for pike-perch
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Cultural heritage of traditional fishing
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Reed harvesting for thatching and bioenergy
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Reed harvesting removes nutrient-rich biomass from the lagoon ecosystem, potentially reducing (not increasing) nutrient levels. Reed beds sequester N and P; their harvest exports these nutrients. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.from: Recreational angling for pike and perch
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Recreational angling has negligible direct contribution to nutrient enrichment compared to agricultural drainage which is the dominant source. Strength reduced to weak, confidence to 2.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Bird-watching and nature tourism have negligible direct contribution to lagoon nutrient enrichment. Visitor numbers are small relative to agricultural nutrient loads. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Eel fishing with fyke nets is a passive, low-impact gear that does not contribute meaningfully to nutrient enrichment. No scientific basis for medium-strength link. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.to: Pike-perch population dynamics
connections.rationale: Microplastic effects on pike-perch populations are poorly documented and likely negative (not positive) through ingestion, inflammation, and endocrine disruption. Limited evidence for population-level effects. Polarity corrected to -, strength to weak, confidence to 2.
references: Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

baltic_estuary.description: Baltic river estuaries and river mouths (e.g., Daugava, Nemunas, Oder). Transitional waters with salinity gradients, migratory fish passages, and legacy industrial contamination.

drivers.name: Salmon and sea trout fishery demand

activities.name: Sand and gravel extraction from river bed

pressures.name: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
Heavy metal contamination from industrial legacy
Physical habitat loss from channelisation
Blocked fish migration routes (dams, weirs)
Altered salinity gradient from flow regulation
Sediment resuspension from dredging
Altered freshwater flow regime from upstream regulation

states.name: Dissolved oxygen levels in stratified waters
Estuarine salinity gradient and mixing zone dynamics
Zostera marina (eelgrass/seagrass) meadow coverage in brackish estuarine waters

impacts.name: Salmon fishery productivity
Nutrient filtration by estuarine wetlands
Nursery function for coastal fish species

welfare.name: Recreational fishing tourism revenue
Port and shipping economic value
Flood damage avoidance for riverside communities

Port and navigation accessibility

Contaminated sediment remediation programmes

connections.from: Salmon and sea trout fishery demand
connections.to: Salmon stocking and hatchery operations
connections.rationale: Demand for salmon fishing drives hatchery stocking programmes to supplement wild runs. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Navigation channel dredging
connections.rationale: Expanding port capacity requires deepening and maintaining navigation channels. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Sediment resuspension from dredging
connections.rationale: Dredging operations directly resuspend contaminated sediments and increase turbidity

connections.to: Heavy metal contamination from industrial legacy
connections.rationale: Ongoing discharges add to legacy contamination in estuarine sediments. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Atlantic salmon migration success
connections.rationale: Dams and weirs block upstream migration, preventing salmon from reaching spawning grounds

connections.to: Dissolved oxygen levels in stratified waters
connections.rationale: Excessive nutrients fuel algal growth; decomposition consumes oxygen in stratified estuary waters. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.to: Salmon fishery productivity
connections.rationale: Successful salmon migration sustains spawning populations and future fishery catches. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Recreational fishing tourism revenue
connections.rationale: Productive salmon runs attract recreational anglers and generate local tourism income. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally

connections.from: Fish passage restoration (dam removal, fish ladders)
connections.to: Blocked fish migration routes (dams, weirs)
connections.rationale: Removing dams or installing fish ladders directly eliminates migration barriers. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.to: Heavy metal contamination from industrial legacy
connections.rationale: Modern treatment removes heavy metals from effluent, reducing ongoing contamination

connections.to: Estuarine benthic invertebrate communities
connections.rationale: Channelisation removes natural substrate and flow variability, reducing benthic habitat quality

connections.to: Nursery function for coastal fish species
connections.rationale: Healthy benthic communities provide prey for juvenile fish in estuarine nurseries. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features (e

connections.from: Salmon and sea trout fishery demand
connections.to: Recreational salmon fishing
connections.rationale: Growing angler demand for salmon and sea trout drives recreational fishing participation and effort in Baltic estuaries
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Altered salinity gradient from flow regulation
connections.rationale: High nutrient loading changes water density and stratification, interacting with flow regulation to alter the estuarine salinity gradient
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Dissolved oxygen levels in stratified waters
connections.to: Estuarine benthic invertebrate communities
connections.rationale: Bottom-water oxygen levels directly control benthic invertebrate survival and community composition in stratified estuaries
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Nursery function for coastal fish species
connections.to: Recreational fishing tourism revenue
connections.rationale: Healthy nursery function sustains fish populations that attract recreational anglers and generate tourism revenue. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Salmon and sea trout fishery demand
connections.rationale: Tourism revenue from recreational fishing reinforces demand for maintaining and enhancing salmon and trout fisheries. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Industrial and municipal wastewater discharge
connections.rationale: River basin nutrient management plans impose discharge limits that reduce industrial and municipal wastewater nutrient loads

connections.to: Navigation channel dredging

connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Salmon stock recovery plans (HELCOM) focus on fish passage restoration, stocking, and fishing regulation, not nutrient reduction. Only indirect link through broader ecosystem management. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Contaminated sediment remediation targets heavy metals and organic pollutants, not nutrient enrichment. May even temporarily increase nutrient release during disturbance. Strength and confidence reduced.

baltic_offshore.description: Open Baltic Sea waters. Semi-enclosed, brackish, with permanent halocline, deep-water hypoxia, and important pelagic fisheries (cod, herring, sprat).

drivers.name: Demand for Baltic cod and herring

activities.name: Pelagic fishing for herring and sprat
Offshore wind farm construction and operation

pressures.name: Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
Underwater noise from shipping and construction
Bycatch of harbour porpoise and seabirds
Seabed disturbance from bottom trawling
Contamination from dumped munitions
Military munition dumping legacy

Zostera marina (seagrass) distribution in nearshore subtidal zones

impacts.name: Commercial fish stock provision (cod, herring, sprat)
Climate regulation through carbon cycling

welfare.name: Fishing fleet economic viability
Energy security from Baltic wind farms
Fish processing industry employment
Maritime trade revenue
Renewable energy generation from offshore wind
Shipping route provision

connections.from: Demand for Baltic cod and herring
connections.to: Demersal trawling for Baltic cod
connections.rationale: Market demand for cod directly drives trawling effort in the Baltic. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
connections.rationale: Excessive trawling effort has driven Eastern Baltic cod below safe biological limits

connections.to: Seabed disturbance from bottom trawling
connections.rationale: Bottom trawls physically disturb soft-sediment seabed habitats. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features (e

connections.to: Bycatch of harbour porpoise and seabirds
connections.rationale: Gillnets and trawls incidentally catch harbour porpoise and diving seabirds. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass
connections.rationale: Chronic overfishing has collapsed the Eastern Baltic cod stock. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Benthic macrofauna in deep basins
connections.rationale: Hypoxic conditions eliminate benthic fauna over large areas of the Baltic deep basins
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass
connections.rationale: Hypoxia reduces cod spawning success and juvenile survival in deep-water spawning areas
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Commercial fish stock provision (cod, herring, sprat)
connections.rationale: Cod biomass directly determines available catch for commercial fisheries. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.from: Commercial fish stock provision (cod, herring, sprat)
connections.to: Fishing fleet economic viability
connections.rationale: Fish stock availability determines whether fishing fleets can operate profitably. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.from: Baltic cod fishing quota reductions (EU TAC)
connections.to: Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
connections.rationale: Quota reductions directly limit fishing mortality on cod stocks. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.from: Trawling closure zones in spawning areas
connections.to: Seabed disturbance from bottom trawling
connections.rationale: Spatial closures prevent trawl damage in ecologically sensitive spawning areas. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features (e

connections.to: Offshore wind farm construction and operation
connections.rationale: Government renewable energy targets drive rapid expansion of offshore wind installations

connections.from: Offshore wind farm construction and operation
connections.to: Underwater noise from shipping and construction
connections.rationale: Pile-driving during construction generates intense underwater noise affecting marine mammals

connections.from: Demand for Baltic cod and herring
connections.to: Pelagic fishing for herring and sprat
connections.rationale: Market demand for herring drives pelagic fishing fleet effort in the Baltic Sea. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Deep-water hypoxia (dead zones)
connections.rationale: Bottom trawling resuspends organic sediments and increases oxygen demand, exacerbating deep-water hypoxia in enclosed basins
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Benthic macrofauna in deep basins
connections.rationale: Deep-water renewal events bring oxygenated water below the halocline, directly controlling benthic macrofauna survival in Baltic basins
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Demand for Baltic cod and herring
connections.rationale: Fleet economic viability sustains political and industry pressure to maintain fish market demand and quota access
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Deep-water hypoxia (dead zones)
connections.rationale: The primary causal direction is hypoxia affecting cod, not overfishing causing hypoxia. While trophic cascade effects are possible, the link from cod stock reduction to deep-water oxygen conditions is very indirect. ICES assessments emphasize hypoxia as driver of cod decline. Strength and confidence reduced.

connections.to: Central Baltic herring recruitment
connections.rationale: Cod collapse released sprat from predation pressure, causing sprat to outcompete herring larvae for zooplankton prey, depressing herring recruitment through competitive exclusion

connections.from: Baltic cod fishing quota reductions (EU TAC)
connections.to: Pelagic fishing for herring and sprat
connections.rationale: Cod quota reductions redirect fishing effort toward pelagic species (herring, sprat), increasing pressure on small pelagic stocks as fishers substitute target species. Note: positive polarity reflects fleet displacement effect - when cod quotas are cut, fishing effort shifts to pelagic species (herring and sprat)

connections.to: Demersal trawling for Baltic cod

connections.to: Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
connections.rationale: Merchant shipping and ferry traffic do not cause overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod. These are independent human activities. No scientific basis for causal link. Strength to weak, confidence to 1.
references: Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Overfishing of Eastern Baltic cod
connections.rationale: Submarine cable and pipeline installation does not cause overfishing of cod. These are independent activities with different environmental pathways. Strength to weak, confidence to 1.
references: Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass
connections.rationale: Contamination from dumped munitions (TNT, heavy metals) would harm cod through toxicological effects, not increase biomass. Polarity corrected from + to -. Effect size is uncertain; strength to weak, confidence to 2.

mediterranean_lagoon.description: Mediterranean coastal lagoons (e.g., Venice Lagoon, Etang de Thau, Mar Menor, Orbetello). Warm, shallow, highly productive systems supporting shellfish farming, traditional fishing, flamingo colonies, and salt pans.

connections.from: Agricultural drainage and irrigation return flows

connections.rationale: Agricultural return flows deliver nitrogen and phosphorus, fuelling macroalgal blooms. Nutrient enrichment from urban wastewater and agricultural runoff stimulates algal blooms, particularly in semi-enclosed lagoons and nearshore waters where residence times are long

connections.to: Anoxic events from decomposing algal biomass
connections.rationale: Mass die-off of macroalgae creates oxygen-depleting decomposition events. Nutrient enrichment from urban wastewater and agricultural runoff stimulates algal blooms, particularly in semi-enclosed lagoons and nearshore waters where residence times are long

connections.from: Anoxic events from decomposing algal biomass
connections.to: Shellfish stock health and condition index
connections.rationale: Anoxia causes mass shellfish mortality events in Mediterranean lagoons. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Seagrass (Zostera noltei, Cymodocea) presence
connections.rationale: Macroalgal mats smother seagrass and algal turbidity reduces light for seagrass growth. Nutrient enrichment from urban wastewater and agricultural runoff stimulates algal blooms, particularly in semi-enclosed lagoons and nearshore waters where residence times are long

connections.from: Shellfish stock health and condition index

connections.to: Agricultural drainage and irrigation return flows
connections.rationale: Protected area designations impose buffer zones and drainage restrictions that limit agricultural discharge to lagoons. connections.to: Agricultural drainage and irrigation return flows
connections.rationale: Intensified agriculture increases irrigation water use and generates larger volumes of nutrient-laden drainage return flows to lagoons

Micheli et al. (2013) "Cumulative human impacts on Mediterranean ecosystems"

connections.to: Eutrophication and macroalgal blooms (Ulva)

connections.to: Eutrophication and macroalgal blooms (Ulva)
connections.rationale: Salt harvesting has minimal nutrient inputs; salt pans concentrate seawater through evaporation with negligible organic matter addition. No published evidence linking artisanal salt harvesting to eutrophication in Mediterranean lagoons.
references: Viaroli et al. (2008) "Community shifts in eutrophic coastal lagoons"

connections.to: Eutrophication and macroalgal blooms (Ulva)

Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"

connections.from: Yacht and sailboat anchoring over Posidonia
connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Each anchor deployment tears up Posidonia shoots and rhizomes. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.rationale: Repeated anchoring creates expanding bare patches in Posidonia meadows; recovery takes decades
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.rationale: Caulerpa spp. colonise damaged areas and outcompete Posidonia in degraded meadows. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.to: Nursery habitat for commercial fish (Sparidae, Labridae)
connections.rationale: Dense Posidonia meadows provide essential nursery habitat for juvenile fish. Seagrass meadows support fish densities 2-10 times higher than unvegetated substrates, serving as nursery habitat for ~20% of the world's largest fisheries

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.to: Blue carbon sequestration in seagrass matte
connections.rationale: Posidonia oceanica matte is among the most effective marine carbon sinks, storing 327 +/- 150 t C/ha with sequestration rates of 7.7-84.4 g C/m2/yr over millennia (Pergent et al. 2014, Fourqurean et al. 2012). Strong causal link well-documented across Mediterranean.
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.to: Sediment stabilisation and coastal protection
connections.rationale: Posidonia root mats bind sediment and leaves attenuate wave energy. Seagrass canopies reduce wave energy by 20-76% and current velocities by 15-65%, promoting sediment stabilization and reducing coastal erosion
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Nursery habitat for commercial fish (Sparidae, Labridae)
connections.to: Artisanal fishery catch value (seagrass-dependent species)
connections.rationale: Loss of nursery habitat reduces recruitment to adult fish populations exploited by fishers. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Anchoring bans in Posidonia protection zones directly prevent physical damage. Studies in Balearic Islands and French Riviera show near-complete elimination of anchor damage within regulated zones (Marba et al. 2014, Pergent-Martini et al. 2015). Strength upgraded from medium.
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Eco-moorings eliminate the need for anchoring, preventing seabed damage. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Sea temperature rise and marine heatwaves
connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.rationale: Marine heatwaves cause shoot mortality at the upper thermal tolerance of Posidonia. Seagrass canopies reduce wave energy by 20-76% and current velocities by 15-65%, promoting sediment stabilization and reducing coastal erosion
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Coastal construction and marina expansion
connections.rationale: Resort and marina development demand drives construction projects that impact nearby Posidonia meadows. Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Hypersaline discharge from desalination
connections.rationale: Turbidity from coastal works and desalination brine discharge are independent pressures with separate causal mechanisms. Co-occurrence does not imply causation. No peer-reviewed evidence for a causal link between these pressures.
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.to: Seagrass-associated fish assemblage
connections.rationale: Posidonia meadow extent and density directly determine the habitat available for seagrass-associated fish assemblages
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Coastal property protection from wave buffering
connections.rationale: Posidonia sediment stabilisation directly reduces storm damage risk and protects coastal properties. Seagrass canopies reduce wave energy by 20-76% and current velocities by 15-65%, promoting sediment stabilization and reducing coastal erosion
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Dive tourism and snorkeling amenity value
connections.to: Recreational boating and yacht tourism demand
connections.rationale: Dive and snorkel amenity value reinforces broader recreational boating and yacht tourism demand in the Mediterranean
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Yacht and sailboat anchoring over Posidonia
connections.rationale: Anchoring bans in Posidonia protection zones directly eliminate yacht anchoring damage in those areas
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Environmental impact assessment for coastal works
connections.to: Coastal construction and marina expansion
connections.rationale: Mandatory EIA requirements can delay, modify, or prevent coastal construction projects that would damage Posidonia meadows
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Demand for sustainable blue carbon credits
connections.to: Yacht and sailboat anchoring over Posidonia
connections.rationale: Blue carbon credit demand incentivizes Posidonia protection, which restricts anchoring. The effect should be negative (reducing anchoring), not positive. However, the pathway is indirect and emerging, so strength is weak.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Yacht and sailboat anchoring over Posidonia
connections.rationale: Water quality requirements (e.g., EU Bathing Water Directive) tend to restrict activities including anchoring near sensitive habitats. The link to increased anchoring is not supported. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.to: Yacht and sailboat anchoring over Posidonia
connections.rationale: No scientific mechanism links climate change adaptation needs to yacht anchoring over Posidonia. Structural connection without ecological basis.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Trawling near seagrass bed margins
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Desalination brine discharge causes osmotic/chemical stress (salinity >39 PSU reduces Posidonia growth; Sanchez-Lizaso et al. 2008) but has no causal link to mechanical anchoring damage. These are independent pressure pathways.
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"

connections.from: Snorkeling and dive tourism over meadows
connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Snorkeling and diving cause minimal physical damage to Posidonia compared to anchoring. Main impact is from dive boat anchoring, not the diving activity itself. The direct link to mechanical anchor damage is tenuous.
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Scientific monitoring surveys increasingly use non-destructive methods (ROVs, underwater video, drone-based assessment). Any trampling impact is negligible compared to recreational anchoring. Strength reduced.
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
connections.rationale: Ocean warming causes Posidonia shoot mortality at upper thermal limits (>28-29C), with marine heatwaves documented to cause mass die-offs (Marba & Duarte 2010, Savva et al. 2018). Polarity corrected from + to - (warming reduces meadow extent).
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Matte (root mat) integrity and accretion rate
connections.to: Nursery habitat for commercial fish (Sparidae, Labridae)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Matte (root mat) integrity and accretion rate
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Nursery habitat for commercial fish (Sparidae, Labridae)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Epiphyte community on seagrass leaves. Seagrass meadows support fish densities 2-10 times higher than unvegetated substrates, serving as nursery habitat for ~20% of the world's largest fisheries
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.to: Nursery habitat for commercial fish (Sparidae, Labridae)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Water clarity over meadow areas
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Water clarity maintenance through particle trapping
connections.to: Dive tourism and snorkeling amenity value
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Water clarity maintenance through particle trapping
references: Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Climate mitigation value of blue carbon storage
connections.to: Dive tourism and snorkeling amenity value
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate mitigation value of blue carbon storage
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Caulerpa invasive species removal programmes. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Mechanical damage from boat anchoring and mooring
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Seagrass transplanting and restoration trials
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Climate change and ocean warming
connections.rationale: Local coastal construction has negligible contribution to global climate change and ocean warming. The GHG emissions from individual construction projects are insignificant at the scale needed to affect sea temperature. Structural bridge connection without scientific basis.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"

connections.to: Climate change and ocean warming
connections.rationale: Hypersaline brine discharge has no causal mechanism linking it to climate change or ocean warming. Energy consumption of desalination plants contributes marginally to GHG emissions but the effect is negligible at the local scale. Structural bridge connection.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Viaroli et al. (2008) "Community shifts in eutrophic coastal lagoons"
Telesca et al. 2015 Marine Pollution Bulletin

mediterranean_rocky_shore.description: Mediterranean rocky shores and sublittoral rocky reefs. Supports sea urchin barrens vs. macroalgal forests, artisanal fishing, and marine reserves with the reserve effect.

drivers.name: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural demand
Sea urchin and shellfish harvesting demand

activities.name: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
Scuba diving tourism on rocky reefs

pressures.name: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
Overharvesting of predatory fish (grouper, dentex)
Mucilage events and marine heatwaves
Ghost fishing from lost nets on rocky substrate
Overharvesting of predatory fish
Physical habitat degradation of rocky substrate
Climate change and ocean warming

impacts.name: Fish provisioning from artisanal rocky reef fishery
Diving and snorkeling tourism attraction
Biodiversity and genetic resource conservation
Scientific and educational value

welfare.name: Artisanal fisher income and cultural continuity
Dive centre and marine tourism revenue
Seafood quality and gastronomic heritage

responses.name: No-take marine reserve designation
Sea urchin harvesting quota management
Spearfishing regulations (ban in reserves)
Lost fishing gear retrieval programmes
Cystoseira habitat restoration projects

connections.to: Sea urchin population density
connections.rationale: Removal of sea urchin predators (fish) triggers urchin population explosion via trophic cascade. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: Dense urchin populations graze macroalgal forests to bare rock (barren grounds). The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.from: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
connections.to: Overharvesting of predatory fish (grouper, dentex)
connections.rationale: Artisanal fishing selectively targets large predatory fish on rocky reefs. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Overharvesting of predatory fish (grouper, dentex)
connections.rationale: The reserve effect on Mediterranean predatory fish is one of the best-documented outcomes in marine ecology. Grouper biomass increases 5-10x inside no-take reserves within 10-15 years (Harmelin et al. 1995, Garcia-Charton et al. 2008, Guidetti & Sala 2007). Multiple studies across >20 Mediterranean MPAs.

connections.to: Diving and snorkeling tourism attraction
connections.rationale: Large predatory fish (especially grouper Epinephelus marginatus) are the primary attraction for dive tourists on Mediterranean reefs. Dive tourism revenue is strongly correlated with large fish sighting rates (Sala et al. 2012, MedPAN reports).

connections.to: Rocky reef biodiversity index
connections.rationale: Macroalgal canopies create habitat complexity supporting diverse invertebrate and fish communities

connections.to: Dive centre and marine tourism revenue
connections.rationale: Diving tourism attraction directly and strongly drives dive centre revenue. This is a direct economic link well-documented in Mediterranean tourism studies (Sala et al. 2012, Badalamenti et al. 2000).

connections.from: Fish provisioning from artisanal rocky reef fishery
connections.to: Artisanal fisher income and cultural continuity
connections.rationale: Fish catches provide the economic basis for artisanal fishing communities. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Managed harvesting can reduce urchin density, allowing macroalgal recovery. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.to: Rocky reef biodiversity index
connections.rationale: Tropical invasives like Siganus compete with native herbivores and alter reef community structure. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification

connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: High sea urchin density causes barren formation by grazing macroalgal canopies to bare rock, a well-documented trophic cascade on Mediterranean rocky reefs

connections.from: Artisanal fisher income and cultural continuity
connections.to: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural demand
connections.rationale: Fisher income viability is a key determinant of whether artisanal fishing traditions persist. Multiple studies document the abandonment of traditional fishing when economic returns decline (Colloca et al. 2017). Upgraded from weak to medium.

connections.to: Fish provisioning from artisanal rocky reef fishery
connections.rationale: Macroalgal canopies support higher fish recruitment and biomass by providing nursery habitat and food web support for commercially targeted species

connections.to: Scuba diving tourism on rocky reefs
connections.rationale: Underwater recreation demand directly drives scuba diving tourism operations on Mediterranean rocky reefs
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Sea urchin population density
connections.rationale: Large predatory fish control sea urchin populations through predation, preventing overgrazing of macroalgae. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: Marine heatwaves and mucilage events cause mass mortality of heat-sensitive Cystoseira and Sargassum canopy species

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Removal of sea urchin predators (grouper, wrasse, sea breams) triggers urchin population explosions that create barren grounds by overgrazing macroalgal canopy

connections.to: Invasive tropical species establishment
connections.rationale: Marine heatwaves weaken native Mediterranean communities and create ecological niches that facilitate establishment of Lessepsian migrants and tropical invasive species

connections.to: Recreational spearfishing
connections.rationale: Marine reserve designation eliminates spearfishing within boundaries, redistributing diving pressure to adjacent unprotected rocky shore areas

connections.to: Overharvesting of predatory fish
connections.rationale: No-take marine reserves eliminate fishing pressure on predatory fish within protected areas. Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas

connections.to: Large predatory fish abundance (grouper, dentex)
connections.rationale: Overharvesting directly reduces predatory fish abundance on rocky shores. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.to: Physical habitat degradation of rocky substrate
connections.rationale: Cystoseira restoration counteracts physical habitat degradation by reestablishing canopy-forming algae

connections.from: Physical habitat degradation of rocky substrate
connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: Physical degradation of rocky substrate reduces macroalgal canopy recruitment and persistence

connections.from: Sea urchin and shellfish harvesting demand
connections.to: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Sea urchin and shellfish harvesting demand
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
connections.rationale: Marine biodiversity conservation goals (MPA designations, EU Habitats Directive) restrict artisanal fishing through spatial closures, gear restrictions, and quotas. The effect is negative (reducing fishing activity), not positive.
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.to: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
connections.rationale: No direct scientific mechanism links climate change adaptation needs to artisanal fishing activity. Structural connection without ecological basis.
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Sea urchin harvesting reduces Paracentrotus lividus density, thereby reducing overgrazing pressure and allowing macroalgal recovery. Large-scale urchin culling has been shown to reduce barren grounds in the Mediterranean (Piazzi & Ceccherelli 2019, Guidetti & Dulcic 2007). Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Scientific monitoring and underwater surveys have no causal mechanism leading to sea urchin overgrazing. Observation does not drive ecological processes. Structural connection.
references: Borja et al. (2010) "Marine management towards an integrated implementation"

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Octopus pot fishing has a negligible direct effect on sea urchin overgrazing. While octopus prey on urchins, pot fishing selectivity and catch levels are insufficient to release significant predation pressure on urchin populations.
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Ghost fishing from lost nets on rocky substrate
connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: Lost fishing nets on rocky substrate cause physical abrasion and smothering of macroalgal canopy. The effect is negative (reducing canopy cover). However, the scale of impact is typically small and localized. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"

connections.to: Macroalgal canopy cover (Cystoseira, Sargassum)
connections.rationale: Ocean warming and marine heatwaves cause mass mortality of heat-sensitive canopy-forming algae (Cystoseira, Sargassum) in the Mediterranean, facilitating replacement by turf algae (Thibaut et al. 2005, Vergés et al. 2014). Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"

connections.to: Fish provisioning from artisanal rocky reef fishery
connections.rationale: Invasive herbivorous fish (Siganus spp.) overgraze macroalgal canopies, reducing habitat for native commercial species. While some invasives are commercially exploited in the eastern Mediterranean, the net effect on traditional artisanal fishery provision is generally negative (Azzurro et al. 2019). Polarity changed to negative.
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"

connections.to: Fish provisioning from artisanal rocky reef fishery
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coastal food web integrity. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Artisanal fisher income and cultural continuity
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Biodiversity and genetic resource conservation
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Artisanal fisher income and cultural continuity
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Scientific and educational value
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural demand
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Seafood quality and gastronomic heritage
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Spearfishing regulations (ban in reserves). Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Overgrazing by sea urchins (urchin barrens)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Lost fishing gear retrieval programmes
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Artisanal trammel net and longline fishing
connections.rationale: Diving tourism demand does not drive artisanal fishing activity. These activities may compete for coastal space, particularly in MPAs. Structural bridge connection without ecological mechanism.
references: Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"
Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"

north_sea_offshore.description: North Sea offshore waters. Highly industrialised shelf sea with oil/gas platforms, major wind farm arrays, sandeel fisheries, aggregate extraction, and busy shipping lanes.

drivers.name: Offshore wind energy policy targets (EU, UK)
Sand and gravel resource demand for construction

activities.name: Oil and gas platform operation and decommissioning

pressures.name: Seabed habitat loss from wind farm foundations
Underwater noise from pile-driving
Sandeel stock depletion affecting seabirds
Chronic hydrocarbon contamination from platforms
Seabed disturbance from beam trawling
Ship-strike and collision risk for marine mammals

states.name: Harbour porpoise and minke whale abundance
Benthic community recovery around wind farms

impacts.name: Fish stock provisioning for human consumption
Seabird conservation value (public concern)

welfare.name: Fishing industry employment and revenue
Energy security and carbon reduction benefits
Decommissioning cost burden (oil sector)
Renewable energy generation capacity
Shipping and trade route services

responses.name: Sandeel fishery closure zones (UK, Denmark)
Maritime spatial planning (North Sea)
Underwater noise mitigation requirements (bubble curtains)
OSPAR regional action plans for marine litter
Platform decommissioning regulations

connections.from: Offshore wind energy policy targets (EU, UK)
connections.to: Offshore wind farm construction (monopile, jacket)
connections.rationale: Government targets directly drive massive investment in offshore wind construction

connections.to: Underwater noise from pile-driving
connections.rationale: Monopile installation generates high-intensity impulsive noise harmful to cetaceans

connections.to: Sandeel stock depletion affecting seabirds
connections.rationale: Industrial sandeel fishing reduces prey availability for surface-feeding seabirds. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Seabird breeding success (kittiwake, puffin, tern)
connections.rationale: Sandeel shortage causes chick starvation and breeding failure in kittiwakes and puffins

connections.to: Sandeel stock abundance and distribution
connections.rationale: Overfishing directly reduces sandeel biomass in the North Sea. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Harbour porpoise and minke whale abundance
connections.rationale: Pile-driving noise causes harbour porpoise displacement and potential hearing damage

connections.to: Seabed disturbance from beam trawling
connections.rationale: Beam trawls physically scrape and flatten seabed habitats. Bottom trawling physically disrupts benthic habitat structure, removing biogenic features (e

connections.to: Fish stock provisioning for human consumption
connections.rationale: Sandeel abundance supports both direct fishery and prey base for commercial fish. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Sandeel stock depletion affecting seabirds
connections.rationale: Closure zones protect sandeel spawning and feeding areas from fishing mortality. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Underwater noise from pile-driving
connections.rationale: Bubble curtains reduce noise propagation during pile-driving by 10-20 dB. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Sandeel industrial fishery
connections.rationale: Industrial demand for fishmeal and fish oil drives sandeel fishery targeting and catch volumes
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.from: Seabed habitat loss from wind farm foundations
connections.to: Seabed disturbance from beam trawling
connections.rationale: Wind farm exclusion zones preventing beam trawling is well-documented in North Sea. Multiple studies confirm fisheries displacement from wind farm areas. Confidence raised from 3 to 4.
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.to: Seabird breeding success (kittiwake, puffin, tern)
connections.rationale: Sandeel availability is the critical prey resource determining breeding success for most North Sea seabird colonies

connections.from: Fish stock provisioning for human consumption
connections.to: Fishing industry employment and revenue
connections.rationale: Fish stock productivity directly determines fishing fleet revenue and coastal community employment. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Industrial-scale fisheries demand (sandeel, herring)
connections.rationale: Fishing industry employment sustains political lobby for maintaining industrial-scale fishery access and quotas

connections.to: Sandeel industrial fishery
connections.rationale: Spatial closure zones directly exclude sandeel fishing from key areas, reducing total industrial catch
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.to: Beam trawling for flatfish (sole, plaice)
connections.rationale: Maritime spatial planning designates areas for wind energy and nature protection that exclude beam trawling
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Offshore wind farm construction (monopile, jacket)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Oil and gas production demand (declining)
references: Emeis et al. (2015) "The North Sea - A shelf sea in the Anthropocene"

north_sea_estuary.description: North Sea estuaries (e.g., Thames, Elbe, Scheldt, Humber). Major port estuaries with heavy industrial legacy, flood defence infrastructure, migratory fish restoration, and high economic importance.

drivers.name: Major port and container terminal expansion
Flood risk management for coastal cities

activities.name: Capital and maintenance dredging for shipping
Tidal flood barrier and sea wall operation
Industrial cooling water abstraction and discharge
Brownfield site remediation on estuarine banks

pressures.name: Legacy contaminants in estuarine sediments (PCBs, metals)
Tidal regime alteration from flood defences
Turbidity maximum zone expansion from dredging
Thermal discharge from power stations
Loss of intertidal habitat from hard defences
Pharmaceutical and endocrine disruptor contamination
Altered freshwater flow regime from upstream regulation

states.name: Dissolved oxygen in turbidity maximum zone
Estuarine salinity gradient and mixing zone dynamics

impacts.name: Flood protection for low-lying areas
Estuarine nursery function for marine fish
Saltmarsh carbon sequestration and wave attenuation

welfare.name: Port and logistics industry employment
Flood damage avoidance for riverside properties
Recreational amenity of estuarine waterfront
Water supply security for industry
Port and navigation accessibility

responses.name: Managed realignment projects (set-back flood defences)
Water Framework Directive improvement measures
Eel Management Plans (EU Eel Regulation)
Contaminated sediment management strategies
Nature-based solutions for flood resilience

connections.from: Major port and container terminal expansion
connections.to: Capital and maintenance dredging for shipping
connections.rationale: Larger vessels require deeper navigation channels, driving continuous dredging. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.from: Capital and maintenance dredging for shipping
connections.to: Turbidity maximum zone expansion from dredging
connections.rationale: Dredging resuspends fine sediment, expanding and intensifying the turbidity maximum

connections.from: Tidal flood barrier and sea wall operation
connections.to: Loss of intertidal habitat from hard defences
connections.rationale: Hard flood defences prevent landward migration of intertidal habitats (coastal squeeze)

connections.from: Loss of intertidal habitat from hard defences
connections.to: Estuarine saltmarsh extent and condition
connections.rationale: Coastal squeeze from sea walls prevents saltmarsh migration, causing net loss. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.from: Legacy contaminants in estuarine sediments (PCBs, metals)
connections.to: European eel recruitment and passage
connections.rationale: PCB bioaccumulation in eels impairs reproduction and reduces population viability. Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas

connections.to: Saltmarsh carbon sequestration and wave attenuation
connections.rationale: Healthy saltmarsh provides natural wave attenuation and blue carbon storage. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Loss of intertidal habitat from hard defences
connections.rationale: Setting back defences creates new intertidal habitat, reversing coastal squeeze. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.from: Turbidity maximum zone expansion from dredging
connections.to: Dissolved oxygen in turbidity maximum zone
connections.rationale: Enhanced turbidity increases oxygen demand from resuspended organic matter. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms

connections.from: Flood risk management for coastal cities
connections.to: Tidal flood barrier and sea wall operation
connections.rationale: Flood risk to coastal populations drives investment in and operation of tidal barriers and sea walls
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Legacy contaminants in estuarine sediments (PCBs, metals)
connections.to: Pharmaceutical and endocrine disruptor contamination
connections.rationale: Legacy contaminant sources indicate industrialized catchments that also generate pharmaceutical and endocrine disruptor pollution
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.to: Estuarine fish community (smelt, flounder, bass)
connections.rationale: Saltmarsh provides essential nursery and feeding habitat for juvenile flatfish, bass and smelt in North Sea estuaries
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.from: Estuarine nursery function for marine fish
connections.to: Port and logistics industry employment
connections.rationale: Healthy estuarine ecosystems supporting fisheries contribute to the broader economic attractiveness of port communities

connections.to: Flood damage avoidance for riverside properties
connections.rationale: Natural and engineered flood protection directly reduces property damage and economic losses for riverside communities
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Flood damage avoidance for riverside properties
connections.to: Flood risk management for coastal cities
connections.rationale: Property owners' experience of flood damage avoidance sustains political demand for continued flood risk management investment
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Industrial cooling water abstraction and discharge
connections.rationale: WFD improvement measures impose thermal discharge limits and ecological flow requirements that constrain industrial water use

connections.to: Managed realignment and saltmarsh creation
connections.rationale: Fish recovery goals drive managed realignment projects that create saltmarsh nursery habitat for migratory species
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.to: Commercial and recreational eel fishing
connections.rationale: The response restricts commercial and recreational eel fishing to allow recovery

caribbean_coral_reef.description: Caribbean coral reef systems. Declining reef health from bleaching, hurricanes, and lionfish invasion. Tourism-dependent economies with small-scale reef fisheries.

drivers.name: Reef tourism and cruise ship visitor demand
Reef fishery dependence for food security

activities.name: Reef snorkeling and scuba diving tourism
Trap and spear fishing on reef
Cruise ship anchoring and wastewater discharge

pressures.name: Mass coral bleaching from thermal stress
Lionfish (Pterois volitans/miles) invasion and predation
Hurricane physical damage to reef structure
Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
Overharvesting of herbivorous reef fish
Coral habitat loss and degradation
Climate change and ocean warming
Global greenhouse gas emissions

states.name: Reef fish species diversity and biomass
Adjacent mangrove forest extent and connectivity to reef system
Seagrass bed coverage between reef and shore

impacts.name: Reef-based tourism attraction value
Coastal wave protection by reef barrier
Reef fishery production for local food supply
Biodiversity and pharmaceutical potential

welfare.name: Tourism-dependent community livelihoods
Food security from reef protein sources
Coastal property protection value
Cultural and spiritual connection to reefs

responses.name: Parrotfish and herbivore harvest bans
Marine Protected Area network expansion
Coral nursery and outplanting programmes
Lionfish culling bounty programmes
Watershed management to reduce runoff

connections.from: Mass coral bleaching from thermal stress
connections.to: Live coral cover percentage
connections.rationale: Severe bleaching causes widespread coral mortality, reducing live coral cover. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Live coral cover percentage
connections.rationale: SCTLD has killed millions of corals across the Caribbean since 2014. The negative feedback reflects a trade-off or antagonistic interaction where increases in the source variable suppress or reduce the target through competitive exclusion, resource limitation, or regulatory mechanisms
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Reef fish species diversity and biomass
connections.rationale: Lionfish consume juvenile reef fish, reducing recruitment and native fish diversity. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Macroalgal cover on reef substrate
connections.rationale: Parrotfish grazing controls macroalgae, preventing algal phase shifts on reefs. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Reef-based tourism attraction value
connections.rationale: High coral cover and reef colour directly determine tourist satisfaction and revisit rates
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Coastal wave protection by reef barrier
connections.rationale: 3D reef structure dissipates wave energy, protecting shorelines from storm damage
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Tourism-dependent community livelihoods
connections.rationale: Reef tourism is the primary economic driver for many Caribbean island communities. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Lionfish (Pterois volitans/miles) invasion and predation
connections.rationale: Targeted culling reduces lionfish density on managed reefs, but recolonisation is rapid

connections.from: Reef tourism and cruise ship visitor demand
connections.to: Reef snorkeling and scuba diving tourism
connections.rationale: Tourism demand directly drives the volume of snorkeling and diving operations on Caribbean coral reefs
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Mass coral bleaching from thermal stress
connections.to: Stony coral tissue loss disease (SCTLD)
connections.rationale: Heat-stressed corals have weakened immune responses, increasing susceptibility to SCTLD infection and spread
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Reef structural complexity (rugosity)
connections.rationale: Living coral growth maintains and builds reef structural complexity, which collapses when corals die and erode
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Reef fishery production for local food supply
connections.to: Food security from reef protein sources
connections.rationale: Reef fish catches are the primary local protein source for many Caribbean coastal communities
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Reef tourism and cruise ship visitor demand
connections.rationale: Communities dependent on tourism revenues actively promote and expand reef tourism to sustain livelihoods. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Trap and spear fishing on reef
connections.rationale: MPA expansion restricts fishing areas and methods, reducing trap and spear fishing pressure on reefs. Well-enforced marine protected areas increase fish biomass by an average of 670%, density by 446%, and species richness by 21% relative to unprotected areas
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Sargassum inundation events
connections.rationale: Elevated nutrient levels from terrestrial runoff fuel pelagic Sargassum blooms and promote macroalgal overgrowth on reef substrate, compounding reef degradation
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Trap and spear fishing on reef
connections.to: Overharvesting of herbivorous reef fish
connections.rationale: Trap and spear fishing on reefs directly targets herbivorous fish including parrotfish, creating overharvesting pressure

connections.to: Parrotfish population (key herbivores)
connections.rationale: Sustained overharvesting depletes parrotfish populations, reducing herbivory on reef algae. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Global greenhouse gas emissions
connections.rationale: The driver 'Climate change and ocean warming' is mediated through global greenhouse gas emissions that alter the climate system
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from thermal stress
connections.rationale: Rising sea temperatures directly trigger mass bleaching when corals exceed thermal thresholds. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Overharvesting of herbivorous reef fish
connections.rationale: Parrotfish harvest bans directly reduce overharvesting pressure on herbivorous reef fish

connections.to: Coral habitat loss and degradation
connections.rationale: Coral nursery programmes and outplanting directly counteract coral habitat loss by restoring live coral framework

connections.to: Live coral cover percentage
connections.rationale: Ongoing coral habitat loss and degradation reduces live coral cover on reef structures

connections.from: Reef fishery dependence for food security
connections.to: Reef snorkeling and scuba diving tourism
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Reef fishery dependence for food security
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Mumby et al. (2006) "Fishing, trophic cascades, and the process of grazing on coral reefs"

arctic_sea_ice.description: Arctic sea ice habitats and ice-edge ecosystems. Rapidly declining due to climate change, affecting ice-dependent species, indigenous communities, and opening new shipping routes.

activities.name: Arctic oil and gas seismic exploration
Climate and sea ice monitoring research

Earlier spring ice breakup disrupting breeding cycles
Oil spill risk in ice-covered waters
Underwater noise from increased shipping
Black carbon deposition accelerating ice melt
