connections.rationale: Reef fish biomass determines the sustainable yield available to artisanal fishers. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.from: Live coral cover (Acropora, Porites, massive faviids)
connections.to: Reef-based coastal wave protection for low-lying islands
connections.rationale: Healthy coral reefs dissipate 97% of wave energy, protecting low-lying atoll nations from flooding
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Reef-based coastal wave protection for low-lying islands
connections.to: Atoll community physical safety from wave overtopping
connections.rationale: Reef wave attenuation is essential for habitability of low-lying Maldivian and Pacific atoll communities
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Reef fishery production for coastal communities
connections.to: Coastal fisher household food security and income
connections.rationale: Reef fishery catches are the primary protein source and income for millions of Indian Ocean coastal households

connections.to: Overfishing of herbivorous reef fish (parrotfish, surgeonfish)
connections.rationale: Parrotfish fishing bans allow herbivore populations to recover, restoring top-down control of macroalgae. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.from: Dynamite fishing enforcement and alternative livelihood programmes
connections.to: Destructive fishing (dynamite and cyanide use)
connections.rationale: Enforcement combined with alternative livelihoods reduces dynamite fishing, though compliance is challenging

connections.from: Reef tourism growth (diving, snorkeling, resort islands)
connections.to: Reef diving and snorkeling tourism operations
connections.rationale: Growing reef tourism demand drives expansion of dive and snorkel operations on Indian Ocean reefs
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Coastal fisher household food security and income
connections.to: Artisanal fishing dependence for coastal food security
connections.rationale: Household-level food security outcomes reinforce community dependence on artisanal fishing as a livelihood strategy

connections.from: Live coral cover (Acropora, Porites, massive faviids)
connections.to: Reef fish biomass and trophic structure
connections.rationale: Live coral provides habitat structure supporting reef fish diversity, biomass, and trophic complexity. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.from: Reef tourism amenity and aesthetic value
connections.to: Dive tourism operator and resort employment
connections.rationale: Reef aesthetic quality directly determines dive tourism patronage and resort employment levels
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.to: Crown-of-thorns starfish (Acanthaster planci) outbreaks
connections.rationale: The causal link between mass bleaching events and CoTS outbreaks is not well-established. CoTS outbreaks are primarily driven by nutrient enrichment and larval supply, not directly by bleaching. Confidence lowered.

connections.from: Overfishing of herbivorous reef fish (parrotfish, surgeonfish)
connections.to: Ocean acidification reducing coral calcification rates
connections.rationale: Overfishing of herbivorous fish does not cause ocean acidification. These are independent stressors driven by different mechanisms (fishing vs. atmospheric CO2). Connection is spurious.

connections.to: Global greenhouse gas emissions
connections.rationale: The driver 'Climate change and ocean warming' is mediated through global greenhouse gas emissions that alter the climate system

connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Indian Ocean warming drives increasingly frequent and severe marine heatwaves that trigger mass coral bleaching. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae

connections.from: Coastal population growth and housing demand
connections.to: Reef diving and snorkeling tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coastal population growth and housing demand
references: Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Global ornamental fish and coral trade demand
connections.to: Reef diving and snorkeling tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Global ornamental fish and coral trade demand
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.to: Reef diving and snorkeling tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate change adaptation needs
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Coral and sand mining for construction material
connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Coral mining causes direct physical reef destruction but does not cause mass bleaching events (which are driven by marine heatwaves). These are independent stressors. Connection conflates different impact pathways.
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
McClanahan et al. (2007) "Effects of climate and seawater temperature variation on coral bleaching"

connections.from: Desalination plant brine and intake operations
connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Desalination brine causes localized thermal and salinity stress but does not trigger mass bleaching events (driven by basin-scale marine heatwaves). Connection conflates local vs. regional stressors.
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Reef mariculture may cause localized stress but does not drive mass coral bleaching events (caused by marine heatwaves from ocean warming). Connection is spurious.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.from: Plastic debris accumulation on reef and beach habitats
connections.to: Live coral cover (Acropora, Porites, massive faviids)
connections.rationale: Plastic debris on reefs increases coral disease likelihood 20-fold (Lamb et al. 2018 Science), causes physical abrasion and smothering. Effect is clearly negative on live coral cover. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.from: Sedimentation from coastal construction and land clearing
connections.to: Live coral cover (Acropora, Porites, massive faviids)
connections.rationale: Sedimentation smothers coral polyps, reduces light penetration, and inhibits larval settlement. This is one of the most well-documented threats to coral reefs globally (Fabricius 2005, Rogers 1990). Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.to: Reef fishery production for coastal communities
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Giant clam (Tridacna spp.) population density. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Reef biodiversity as genetic and pharmaceutical resource
connections.to: Coastal fisher household food security and income
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Reef biodiversity as genetic and pharmaceutical resource
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Cultural and spiritual value of reef ecosystems
connections.to: Reef tourism growth (diving, snorkeling, resort islands)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Cultural and spiritual value of reef ecosystems
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Marine Protected Area networks (e.g., Maldives Baa Atoll Biosphere)
connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: MPAs cannot prevent mass bleaching events (driven by ocean warming), but they may enhance reef resilience and recovery capacity by reducing local stressors. Direct effect on bleaching pressure is weak (Selig et al. 2012).
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Coral reef restoration (coral gardening, larval reseeding)
connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coral reef restoration (coral gardening, larval reseeding)
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
McClanahan et al. (2007) "Effects of climate and seawater temperature variation on coral bleaching"

connections.from: Climate adaptation planning for reef-dependent nations
connections.to: Mass coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate adaptation planning for reef-dependent nations. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.to: Artisanal reef fishing (traps, handlines, spearfishing)
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Dive tourism operator and resort employment
connections.to: Artisanal fishing dependence for coastal food security
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Artisanal reef fishing (traps, handlines, spearfishing)
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

mediterranean_island.description: Mediterranean insular systems (Balearic Islands, Cyclades, Sardinia, Crete, Malta). Characterised by seasonal tourism population explosions, limited freshwater, Posidonia oceanica meadows fringing island shelves, artisanal fishing traditions, and waste management challenges amplified by geographic isolation.

drivers.name: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural heritage

activities.name: Artisanal trammel net fishing for demersal species
Ferry and cruise ship port operations

pressures.name: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
Coastal waste accumulation from limited island disposal capacity
Seawater intake and brine discharge from desalination
Tourist overcrowding of beaches and nearshore waters
Invasive mammalian predators (rats, feral cats) on native wildlife
Climate change and ocean warming

states.name: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent around island shelves
Coastal bathing water quality (E. coli, enterococci)
Demersal fish stock biomass (Mullus, Diplodus, Sparisoma)
Native seabird colony breeding success (Audouin's gull, shearwaters)
Invasive Caulerpa cylindracea displacement of native habitats

impacts.name: Fish provisioning from artisanal island fisheries
Coastal protection by Posidonia banquette deposits
Aesthetic and recreational value of clear island waters
Carbon sequestration by Posidonia matte

welfare.name: Tourism industry employment and island household income
Fisher livelihoods from local seafood markets and restaurants
Cultural identity and sense of place for island communities
Resident quality of life affected by seasonal overcrowding

responses.name: EU MSFD Good Environmental Status targets for island waters
Mooring buoy systems to prevent Posidonia anchor damage
Tourist carrying capacity regulations for beaches and coves
Posidonia oceanica habitat restoration programmes
Seasonal fishing closures for spawning protection
Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes

connections.to: Beach and yacht tourism operations
connections.rationale: Increasing Mediterranean tourism demand drives expansion of beach services and yacht charter operations on islands

connections.to: Coastal resort and marina construction
connections.rationale: Tourism demand drives new resort and marina development on island coastlines. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Summer population surges (often 5-10x resident numbers) overwhelm island wastewater treatment capacity

connections.to: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.rationale: Yacht and pleasure boat anchors physically uproot Posidonia shoots, creating expanding dead patches around island anchorages

connections.to: Seawater intake and brine discharge from desalination
connections.rationale: Desalination plants discharge hypersaline brine that locally elevates salinity and reduces dissolved oxygen

connections.from: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.to: Coastal bathing water quality (E. coli, enterococci)
connections.rationale: Untreated or poorly treated sewage degrades nearshore bathing water quality around island beaches. Tourism-generated wastewater (sewage, greywater) degrades nearshore water quality, particularly during peak season when treatment capacity is exceeded

connections.from: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent around island shelves
connections.rationale: Cumulative anchor scars fragment meadows; Posidonia grows only 1-2 cm/year so recovery takes decades

connections.from: Invasive Caulerpa cylindracea displacement of native habitats
connections.to: Infralittoral rocky reef algal assemblages
connections.rationale: Caulerpa cylindracea outcompetes native algae on rocky substrates, reducing diversity. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent around island shelves
connections.to: Coastal protection by Posidonia banquette deposits
connections.rationale: Healthy Posidonia meadows produce leaf litter banquettes that protect island beaches from storm erosion. Seagrass canopies reduce wave energy by 20-76% and current velocities by 15-65%, promoting sediment stabilization and reducing coastal erosion
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Coastal bathing water quality (E. coli, enterococci)
connections.to: Aesthetic and recreational value of clear island waters
connections.rationale: Clean, clear waters are the primary attraction for island tourism and recreation. Tourism-generated wastewater (sewage, greywater) degrades nearshore water quality, particularly during peak season when treatment capacity is exceeded

connections.from: Aesthetic and recreational value of clear island waters
connections.to: Tourism industry employment and island household income
connections.rationale: Water quality directly determines tourist satisfaction, repeat visits, and island tourism revenue. Tourism-generated wastewater (sewage, greywater) degrades nearshore water quality, particularly during peak season when treatment capacity is exceeded

connections.from: Fish provisioning from artisanal island fisheries
connections.to: Fisher livelihoods from local seafood markets and restaurants
connections.rationale: Artisanal catches supply local restaurants and markets, sustaining fisher household income

connections.from: Tourism industry employment and island household income
connections.to: Mass seasonal tourism demand
connections.rationale: Economic dependence on tourism reinforces promotion and expansion of tourism demand (feedback loop). Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally

connections.from: Mooring buoy systems to prevent Posidonia anchor damage
connections.to: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.rationale: Mooring buoys eliminate anchoring in sensitive Posidonia areas, halting physical damage

connections.from: Tourist carrying capacity regulations for beaches and coves
connections.to: Tourist overcrowding of beaches and nearshore waters
connections.rationale: Access limits reduce peak visitor numbers, though enforcement is challenging on islands with many coves

connections.from: Resident quality of life affected by seasonal overcrowding
connections.to: Mass seasonal tourism demand
connections.rationale: Declining resident quality of life generates political pressure for tourism limits, a balancing feedback

connections.from: Cultural identity and sense of place for island communities
connections.to: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural heritage
connections.rationale: Cultural identity rooted in island traditions reinforces community demand for preserving artisanal fishing heritage

connections.from: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.to: Invasive Caulerpa cylindracea displacement of native habitats
connections.rationale: Sewage-driven nutrient enrichment creates conditions favouring fast-growing invasive Caulerpa over native seagrass. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, feral cats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Native seabird colony breeding success (Audouin's gull, shearwaters)
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators depredate eggs, chicks, and nesting adults, severely reducing seabird breeding success on mediterranean island systems

connections.from: Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes
connections.to: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, feral cats) on native wildlife
connections.rationale: Systematic eradication and ongoing biosecurity measures remove invasive predators, allowing native wildlife recovery on islands

connections.to: Coastal waste accumulation from limited island disposal capacity
connections.rationale: Tourism and island activities generate waste volumes that exceed limited island disposal and recycling capacity, particularly during peak season

connections.from: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent around island shelves
connections.to: Demersal fish stock biomass (Mullus, Diplodus, Sparisoma)
connections.rationale: Posidonia meadows serve as essential nursery and feeding habitat for many demersal fish species; meadow loss directly reduces juvenile recruitment and adult fish biomass around islands

connections.to: Endemic island fish community composition
connections.rationale: Algal canopy on rocky reefs provides three-dimensional habitat structure and food sources; canopy loss simplifies habitat and reduces endemic fish species that depend on canopy-associated prey

connections.from: Tourist carrying capacity regulations for beaches and coves
connections.to: Recreational boat anchoring in coves
connections.rationale: Beach and cove carrying capacity limits reduce vessel anchoring pressure in popular bays, redistributing tourism activity to less sensitive areas

connections.to: Beach and yacht tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Marine transport connectivity to mainland
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Beach and yacht tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Freshwater scarcity and desalination needs

connections.to: Beach and yacht tourism operations
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate change adaptation needs
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"

connections.from: Artisanal trammel net fishing for demersal species
connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Artisanal fishing has no causal mechanism linking it to sewage overload. Sewage is driven by population density during tourist season, not by fishing activities. Structural connection.
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Ferry and cruise ship port operations
connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Ferry and cruise ship port operations
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent around island shelves
connections.rationale: Ocean warming and marine heatwaves cause Posidonia shoot mortality around island shelves, particularly at the upper thermal tolerance limit (Marba & Duarte 2010). Mediterranean island Posidonia meadows are among the most vulnerable to climate change. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Coll et al. (2012) "The Mediterranean Sea under siege"

connections.to: Tourism industry employment and island household income
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Carbon sequestration by Posidonia matte. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.from: EU MSFD Good Environmental Status targets for island waters
connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for EU MSFD Good Environmental Status targets for island waters
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"

connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Posidonia restoration programmes have no mechanism to reduce sewage overload. Sewage management requires wastewater treatment infrastructure, not seagrass restoration. Structural connection.
references: Lotze et al. (2011) "Recovery of marine animal populations"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.from: Seasonal fishing closures for spawning protection
connections.to: Sewage overload during peak tourist season
connections.rationale: Seasonal fishing closures for spawning protection have no causal mechanism to reduce sewage overload during tourist season. These address different environmental pressures. Structural connection.
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.to: Coastal bathing water quality (E. coli, enterococci)
connections.rationale: Anchor damage to Posidonia has a very indirect effect on bathing water quality. While Posidonia meadows contribute to water clarity, the link from anchor damage to bacterial contamination (E. coli, enterococci) is not scientifically supported.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, feral cats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.rationale: No ecological mechanism links terrestrial invasive mammalian predators to underwater anchor damage on Posidonia meadows. These are completely independent pressure pathways. Structural bridge connection.
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.from: Fish provisioning from artisanal island fisheries
connections.to: Resident quality of life affected by seasonal overcrowding
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Artisanal fishing tradition and cultural heritage
connections.to: Artisanal trammel net fishing for demersal species
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Anchor damage to Posidonia oceanica meadows
connections.rationale: Desalination brine discharge causes osmotic stress to Posidonia (Sanchez-Lizaso et al. 2008) but has no mechanism to cause mechanical anchor damage. These are independent pressure pathways. Structural bridge connection.
references: Coll et al. (2010) "The biodiversity of the Mediterranean Sea"

caribbean_island.description: Caribbean Small Island Developing States (Barbados, Jamaica, Lesser Antilles, Bahamas, Trinidad). High tourism dependency, coral reef fringing islands, hurricane vulnerability, Sargassum inundation events, and SIDS-specific governance challenges including food import dependency and limited waste infrastructure.

drivers.name: Food import dependency and local food security

activities.name: Cruise ship port calls and excursions
Sugar cane and rum production (agricultural runoff)
Coral reef diving and snorkelling tourism

pressures.name: Coral bleaching from marine heatwaves
Hurricane physical damage to reefs and coastline
Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
Plastic and solid waste pollution from limited disposal
Overfishing of reef herbivores (parrotfish, surgeonfish)
Invasive mammalian predators (rats, mongoose) on native wildlife
Saltwater intrusion from sea level rise and over-extraction
Hurricane formation and intensification

states.name: Fringing coral reef live cover and structural complexity
Reef fish community biomass and trophic structure
Native seabird colony breeding success (terns, boobies, frigatebirds)

impacts.name: Reef-based tourism and recreation value
Coastal storm protection by coral reefs and mangroves
Fish protein provisioning for local food security
Nursery habitat for commercially important species

welfare.name: Tourism-dependent household income and employment
Fisher community livelihoods and food access
Coastal community safety from storm events
National economic resilience (SIDS GDP vulnerability)

responses.name: Coral reef marine protected area networks
Parrotfish fishing bans for herbivore protection
SIDS climate adaptation and resilience programmes
Sargassum early warning and beach clean-up systems
Mangrove restoration for coastal defence
Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes

connections.to: All-inclusive resort tourism operations
connections.rationale: SIDS economic dependency on tourism drives continuous resort expansion and visitor promotion. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Cruise ship port calls and excursions
connections.rationale: Growing cruise industry drives more port calls and onshore excursion activities. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Overfishing of reef herbivores (parrotfish, surgeonfish)
connections.rationale: Unregulated trap fishing removes herbivorous fish that maintain reef health through grazing

connections.from: Sugar cane and rum production (agricultural runoff)
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Agricultural clearing and fertiliser use on steep island slopes delivers nutrients and sediment to nearshore reefs
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Fringing coral reef live cover and structural complexity
connections.rationale: Mass bleaching events cause widespread coral mortality, reducing live cover and 3D structural complexity
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Fringing coral reef live cover and structural complexity
connections.rationale: Removal of herbivores allows macroalgae to overgrow corals, shifting reefs to algal-dominated states

connections.to: Seagrass (Thalassia testudinum) bed coverage
connections.rationale: Decomposing Sargassum smothers nearshore seagrass beds and creates anoxic conditions
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Fringing coral reef live cover and structural complexity
connections.to: Reef-based tourism and recreation value
connections.rationale: Healthy, colourful reefs with abundant fish are the primary attraction for dive and snorkel tourism

connections.from: Fringing coral reef live cover and structural complexity
connections.to: Coastal storm protection by coral reefs and mangroves
connections.rationale: Structurally complex reefs dissipate up to 97% of wave energy, protecting island coastlines. Mangrove forests reduce wave height by 13-66% per 100 m of forest width, depending on tree density and tidal conditions
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: Tourism-dependent household income and employment
connections.rationale: Reef tourism generates the majority of island tourism revenue and employment. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Coastal storm protection by coral reefs and mangroves
connections.to: Coastal community safety from storm events
connections.rationale: Natural coastal defences reduce storm surge damage and protect lives and property. Mangrove forests reduce wave height by 13-66% per 100 m of forest width, depending on tree density and tidal conditions
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.to: International tourism dependency for GDP
connections.rationale: Economic success from tourism reinforces national dependency on this sector, creating a lock-in feedback loop. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Parrotfish fishing bans for herbivore protection
connections.to: Overfishing of reef herbivores (parrotfish, surgeonfish)
connections.rationale: Banning parrotfish harvest allows herbivore recovery, restoring grazing pressure on macroalgae

connections.from: SIDS climate adaptation and resilience programmes
connections.to: Hurricane physical damage to reefs and coastline
connections.rationale: Building codes, early warning systems, and reef restoration reduce vulnerability to hurricane damage
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Fisher community livelihoods and food access
connections.to: Food import dependency and local food security
connections.rationale: Improved fisher livelihoods reduce dependency on food imports, but declining fisheries increase it (feedback)

connections.from: Fisher community livelihoods and food access
connections.to: Coastal livelihoods and poverty reduction
connections.rationale: Fisher community welfare outcomes reinforce political demands for coastal livelihood support and poverty reduction programmes
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Hurricane physical damage to reefs and coastline
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Hurricane damage strips vegetation cover from hillslopes, dramatically increasing subsequent nutrient and sediment runoff
references: Burke et al. 2011 Reefs at Risk Revisited

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, mongoose) on native wildlife
connections.to: Native seabird colony breeding success (terns, boobies, frigatebirds)
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators depredate eggs, chicks, and nesting adults, severely reducing seabird breeding success on caribbean island systems

connections.from: Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes
connections.to: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, mongoose) on native wildlife
connections.rationale: Systematic eradication and ongoing biosecurity measures remove invasive predators, allowing native wildlife recovery on islands

connections.to: Plastic and solid waste pollution from limited disposal
connections.rationale: Tourism and island activities generate waste volumes that exceed limited island disposal and recycling capacity, particularly during peak season

connections.to: Coral bleaching from marine heatwaves

pacific_island_atoll.description: Pacific low-lying atoll nations (Tuvalu, Marshall Islands, Kiribati, Tokelau) and atoll systems in Fiji and Micronesia. Facing existential sea level rise threats, dependent on reef fisheries for protein, traditional marine tenure systems (taboos), freshwater lens vulnerability, and tuna licensing as primary revenue source.

Tuna access fee revenue for national budgets
Traditional ocean governance and cultural identity
Population growth on limited land area

Foreign fleet tuna fishing under access agreements

Global greenhouse gas emissions

connections.from: Food security from reef and lagoon fisheries
connections.to: Subsistence reef gleaning and spearfishing
connections.rationale: Dependence on reef protein as the primary food source drives daily subsistence harvesting on atoll reefs

connections.to: Overharvesting of reef invertebrates (giant clams, sea cucumbers)
connections.rationale: Growing populations increase harvest pressure on slow-growing invertebrates beyond sustainable levels. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.from: Sea level rise from thermal expansion and ice melt
connections.to: Freshwater lens volume and salinity
connections.rationale: Rising sea levels and overwash events contaminate freshwater lenses with saltwater, shrinking potable supply. Global mean sea level rise (currently ~3

connections.to: Atoll reef crest structural integrity
connections.rationale: Bleaching-induced coral mortality weakens reef crest structure, reducing its capacity to keep pace with sea level rise. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae

connections.to: Lagoon water quality and coral cover
connections.rationale: IPCC AR6 assesses with high confidence that ocean acidification reduces coral calcification rates and reef accretion, directly threatening lagoon coral cover. Effect is strong and well-documented (Hoegh-Guldberg et al. 2017).

connections.to: Coastal protection by reef crest wave dissipation
connections.rationale: Intact reef crests dissipate >90% of wave energy; degraded crests allow destructive waves to reach shore
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Reef fish abundance for subsistence harvest
connections.to: Reef fish protein provisioning for atoll communities
connections.rationale: Fish abundance directly determines the catch available for household consumption on atolls

connections.from: Reef fish protein provisioning for atoll communities
connections.to: Household food security and nutrition
connections.rationale: Reef fish provide 50-90% of dietary animal protein for atoll populations. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.from: Coastal protection by reef crest wave dissipation
connections.to: Community safety from coastal inundation
connections.rationale: Wave energy reduction by reef crests prevents overwash and protects island settlements
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Food security from reef and lagoon fisheries
connections.rationale: Declining food security reinforces the driver for more reef fishing, creating a potential overharvest feedback

connections.from: Traditional marine tenure and seasonal taboo systems
connections.to: Overharvesting of reef invertebrates (giant clams, sea cucumbers)
connections.rationale: Traditional taboos on harvesting certain areas or species allow stocks to recover during closed periods

connections.to: Subsistence reef gleaning and spearfishing
connections.rationale: Managed areas restrict harvest in core zones, redistributing fishing effort to sustainable levels

connections.to: Traditional ocean governance and cultural identity
connections.rationale: Strong cultural identity sustains traditional governance systems that support ocean stewardship (feedback)
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Freshwater lens protection and rainwater harvesting
connections.to: Freshwater lens salinisation from overwash events
connections.rationale: Rainwater harvesting reduces groundwater extraction and lens protection measures limit salinisation pathways

connections.to: Food security from reef and lagoon fisheries
connections.rationale: Cultural continuity reinforces traditional reliance on reef fisheries for community food security

connections.from: Sea level rise from thermal expansion and ice melt
connections.to: Freshwater lens salinisation from overwash events
connections.rationale: Sea level rise increases frequency and severity of overwash events that salinise the freshwater lens. Global mean sea level rise (currently ~3
references: Poloczanska et al. 2013 Nature Climate Change
Nurse et al. 2014 IPCC AR5 Chapter 29

connections.to: Ocean acidification reducing coral calcification
connections.rationale: Coral bleaching and ocean acidification are parallel consequences of rising CO2 and ocean warming, not causally linked (bleaching does not cause acidification). They co-occur but are driven by the same root cause. Causal link is spurious.

connections.to: Freshwater lens volume and salinity
connections.rationale: Intact reef crests dissipate wave energy protecting atolls from overwash events; reef crest degradation increases wave overtopping frequency, salinising the freshwater lens through a tipping point mechanism

connections.to: Global greenhouse gas emissions

references: Poloczanska et al. 2013 Nature Climate Change

connections.to: Sea level rise from thermal expansion and ice melt

atlantic_island.description: Macaronesian and mid-Atlantic volcanic island systems (Canary Islands, Azores, Madeira, Cape Verde). Characterised by volcanic substrate geology, deepwater upwelling productivity, seamount-associated pelagic fisheries, cetacean migration corridors, nature-based tourism, and Laurel forest-to-ocean ecological connectivity.

Deep-water fisheries for tuna and swordfish

indian_ocean_island.description: Tropical Indian Ocean island states (Seychelles, Mauritius, Comoros, Réunion). High endemism on granitic and coralline islands, luxury tourism sector, industrial tuna processing, Seychelles blue economy model, coral bleaching vulnerability, and small territorial waters relative to EEZ.

drivers.name: Luxury tourism and resort development demand

pressures.name: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
Sedimentation from upland deforestation and agriculture
Poaching of sea turtles and giant tortoises
Coastal erosion from reef degradation
Invasive mammalian predators (rats, cats, tenrecs) on native wildlife
Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
Saltwater intrusion from sea level rise and over-extraction
Climate change and ocean warming
Global greenhouse gas emissions

Seabird colony sizes (sooty tern, white tern, frigatebird)
Sea turtle nesting success (hawksbill, green turtle)
Reef fish assemblage diversity and biomass
Native seabird colony breeding success (sooty tern, white tern, frigatebird)
Invasive species threatening endemic island biota

Artisanal fish protein provisioning
Biodiversity and endemism conservation value

Tuna industry worker livelihoods

IOTC tuna stock management measures

connections.from: Luxury tourism and resort development demand
connections.to: Luxury resort and eco-lodge tourism
connections.rationale: International demand for luxury tropical holidays drives resort construction and operation on Indian Ocean islands

connections.to: Industrial purse seine tuna processing (canning)
connections.rationale: Global tuna market demand sustains large-scale processing operations in Seychelles and Mauritius

connections.to: Sedimentation from upland deforestation and agriculture
connections.rationale: Resort construction on hillsides and coastal clearing increases sediment runoff to nearshore reefs

connections.from: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.to: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.rationale: Bleaching causes mass coral mortality; granitic reefs show better recovery than coralline reefs due to structural complexity

connections.to: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.rationale: CoTS outbreaks in Indian Ocean islands (Seychelles) are less frequent and severe than in the Great Barrier Reef. Effect on reef condition is moderate. Strength changed from strong to medium.

connections.from: Sedimentation from upland deforestation and agriculture
connections.to: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.rationale: Sediment plumes smother coral, reduce light, and inhibit larval settlement on nearshore reefs

connections.from: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.to: Pristine reef and beach tourism experience value
connections.rationale: Healthy reefs with clear water and abundant marine life are the core attraction for luxury island tourism
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Sea turtle nesting success (hawksbill, green turtle)
connections.to: Biodiversity and endemism conservation value
connections.rationale: Successful turtle nesting is an indicator of overall ecosystem health and conservation effectiveness

connections.from: Pristine reef and beach tourism experience value
connections.to: Tourism-sector employment and household income
connections.rationale: The pristine image directly sustains the premium tourism market and associated employment. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Luxury tourism and resort development demand
connections.rationale: Economic success reinforces government and private sector investment in tourism promotion (feedback loop). Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Seychelles Marine Spatial Plan and blue bonds
connections.to: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.rationale: MSP designates 30% of waters as protected, building reef resilience to bleaching through reduced local stressors. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: National pride in blue economy leadership
connections.to: Blue economy diversification strategy
connections.rationale: International recognition of blue economy achievements reinforces political commitment to the strategy (feedback)

connections.to: Artisanal trap and handline fishing
connections.rationale: Food security needs directly drive artisanal trap and handline fishing effort on Indian Ocean islands
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.to: Food security from artisanal fisheries
connections.rationale: Community food access outcomes reinforce the demand for maintaining artisanal fisheries as a food security strategy
references: FAO 2020 State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture

connections.from: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.to: Coastal erosion from reef degradation
connections.rationale: Bleaching-induced coral mortality reduces reef structure, diminishing wave attenuation and accelerating coastal erosion. Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Obura et al. 2017 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, cats, tenrecs) on native wildlife
connections.to: Native seabird colony breeding success (sooty tern, white tern, frigatebird)
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators depredate eggs, chicks, and nesting adults, severely reducing seabird breeding success on indian ocean island systems

connections.from: Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes
connections.to: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, cats, tenrecs) on native wildlife
connections.rationale: Systematic eradication and ongoing biosecurity measures remove invasive predators, allowing native wildlife recovery on islands

connections.to: Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
connections.rationale: Tourism and island activities generate waste volumes that exceed limited island disposal and recycling capacity, particularly during peak season

connections.from: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.to: Reef fish assemblage diversity and biomass
connections.rationale: Healthy coral cover on granitic and coralline reefs provides the structural complexity essential for maintaining diverse reef fish assemblages in island waters

connections.to: Sea turtle nesting success (hawksbill, green turtle)
connections.rationale: Mangrove and coastal wetland systems provide foraging habitat for juvenile sea turtles; their extent influences turtle population recruitment and nesting beach stability

connections.to: Artisanal trap and handline fishing
connections.rationale: Industrial tuna fishing restrictions under IOTC measures shift fishing pressure toward artisanal methods and nearshore reef resources as alternative livelihood sources. Note: positive polarity reflects effort displacement - industrial tuna management restrictions redirect fishing effort toward artisanal trap and handline methods

connections.to: Global greenhouse gas emissions
connections.rationale: The driver 'Climate change and ocean warming' is mediated through global greenhouse gas emissions that alter the climate system
references: Obura et al. 2017 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.to: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.rationale: The thermal inertia of the Indian Ocean means GHG-driven warming manifests as mass bleaching events on decadal timescales, not short-term. Changed from short-term to long-term per IPCC AR6 ocean heat content projections.

connections.to: Luxury resort and eco-lodge tourism
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate change adaptation needs
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.from: Poaching of sea turtles and giant tortoises
connections.to: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.rationale: Poaching does not improve reef condition. Hawksbill turtles feed on sponges that compete with corals, so turtle removal could indirectly harm reefs. Effect is negative but weak and indirect. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Obura et al. (2017) "Coral reef status report for the Western Indian Ocean"

connections.from: Saltwater intrusion from sea level rise and over-extraction
connections.to: Granitic reef vs coralline reef condition and cover
connections.rationale: Saltwater intrusion primarily affects terrestrial freshwater resources. Any reef effect would be through altered groundwater discharge (submarine groundwater can carry nutrients/pollutants). Polarity corrected; effect is weak and indirect.
references: Obura et al. (2017) "Coral reef status report for the Western Indian Ocean"
Obura et al. 2017 Marine Pollution Bulletin

connections.from: Seabird colony sizes (sooty tern, white tern, frigatebird)

connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Seabird colony sizes (sooty tern, white tern, frigatebird)
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Freshwater lens integrity and volume
references: Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Invasive species threatening endemic island biota
connections.to: Pristine reef and beach tourism experience value
connections.rationale: Invasive species degrade the pristine natural environment that attracts tourists to island destinations. Loss of endemic species reduces ecotourism appeal. Effect is clearly negative. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Tourism-sector employment and household income
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Artisanal fish protein provisioning. Marine tourism generates substantial economic value — reef tourism alone contributes ~USD 36 billion/year globally
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

Sea surface temperature (SST) exceeding the local bleaching threshold (typically 1°C above the maximum monthly mean for ≥4 weeks, measured as Degree Heating Weeks) triggers expulsion of symbiotic zooxanthellae
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"

connections.from: Invasive predator eradication on seabird islands
connections.to: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.rationale: Invasive predator eradication on islands has no direct effect on coral bleaching events (driven by marine heatwaves). Seabird recovery may indirectly benefit reef nutrient cycling but does not affect thermal stress. Connection is spurious.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Obura et al. (2017) "Coral reef status report for the Western Indian Ocean"

connections.to: Coral bleaching from Indian Ocean warming events
connections.rationale: Turtle nesting beach protection does not affect coral bleaching events (driven by ocean warming). These are independent conservation measures. Connection is spurious.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Burke et al. (2011) "Reefs at Risk Revisited"
Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (rats, cats, tenrecs) on native wildlife
connections.to: Climate change and ocean warming
connections.rationale: Invasive predators on islands do not drive global climate change and ocean warming. These are independent processes. Connection is spurious.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
McClanahan et al. (2007) "Effects of climate and seawater temperature variation on coral bleaching"
Obura et al. 2017 Marine Pollution Bulletin

baltic_island.description: Baltic Sea island systems (Gotland, Saaremaa, Åland, Bornholm, Rügen). Characterised by low salinity (5-8 PSU), seasonal ice cover, coastal meadow habitats, grey seal haul-outs, medieval heritage tourism, eutrophication from mainland rivers, and cyanobacteria bloom exposure. EU MSFD and HELCOM governance framework.

drivers.name: Seasonal tourism and cultural heritage visitation
Traditional Baltic fishing and farming livelihoods
Wind energy development in shallow island waters
Agricultural production and food supply demand

activities.name: Offshore wind farm construction and operation

pressures.name: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
Recreational boating disturbance to breeding seabirds
Winter ice reduction affecting ice-dependent species
Invasive mammalian predators (American mink, rats) on native wildlife
Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
Coastal farmland drainage to nearshore waters

states.name: Coastal meadow breeding bird populations (waders, geese)
Strandflat Fucus and filamentous algal mat extent
Herring and perch stock condition around islands
Nearshore water clarity and Secchi depth
Native seabird colony breeding success (eider, terns, guillemots)
Invasive round goby altering nearshore food webs
Grey seal predation on coastal fish stocks and gear damage
Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
Seagrass (Zostera marina) beds in brackish shallow island waters

impacts.name: Recreational and aesthetic value of island beaches
Fish provisioning from small-scale island fisheries
Coastal meadow biodiversity and grazing landscape heritage
Seal watching and nature tourism attraction

welfare.name: Tourism and hospitality employment on islands
Fisher household income from herring and perch sales
Resident quality of life during summer algal bloom events
Cultural identity linked to island maritime heritage

responses.name: HELCOM Baltic Sea Action Plan nutrient targets
Coastal meadow grazing restoration schemes
Grey seal management plans (population control debate)
EU MSFD Good Environmental Status indicators for islands
Seabird nesting island access restrictions
Invasive predator control and biosecurity programmes

connections.from: Seasonal tourism and cultural heritage visitation
connections.to: Recreational boating and sailing
connections.rationale: Summer tourism drives high volumes of recreational boats around Baltic islands. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Coastal farmland drainage to nearshore waters
connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Nutrient drainage from island farms and mainland rivers fuels cyanobacteria blooms that wash onto island shores. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.to: Recreational boating disturbance to breeding seabirds
connections.rationale: Boats approaching nesting islets disturb breeding seabirds, causing nest abandonment and chick mortality
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.to: Strandflat Fucus and filamentous algal mat extent
connections.rationale: Eutrophication shifts algal communities from perennial Fucus to ephemeral filamentous mats. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.from: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.to: Nearshore water clarity and Secchi depth
connections.rationale: Nutrient enrichment increases phytoplankton and reduces water transparency around island coasts. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.from: Grey seal predation on coastal fish stocks and gear damage
connections.to: Herring and perch stock condition around islands
connections.rationale: Stockholm University research shows grey seal predation impacts fish stocks less than fisheries, climate change, and nutrient loads. At population size of 30,000, seal consumption affects fish biomass less than other factors. Strength reduced to weak.

connections.from: Nearshore water clarity and Secchi depth
connections.to: Recreational and aesthetic value of island beaches
connections.rationale: Clear water is essential for island beach tourism; algal blooms and turbidity deter visitors
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Herring and perch stock condition around islands
connections.to: Fish provisioning from small-scale island fisheries
connections.rationale: Healthy fish stocks sustain catches for island fishers supplying local markets. Fishing mortality exceeding maximum sustainable yield (Fmsy) reduces spawning stock biomass below safe biological limits

connections.to: Seal watching and nature tourism attraction
connections.rationale: Large seal haul-out colonies attract wildlife tourists to island boat excursions
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Recreational and aesthetic value of island beaches
connections.to: Tourism and hospitality employment on islands
connections.rationale: Beach and nature quality directly determines tourist visitor numbers and tourism sector employment
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Fish provisioning from small-scale island fisheries
connections.to: Fisher household income from herring and perch sales
connections.rationale: Fish catches directly translate to fisher income in island communities. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.from: Resident quality of life during summer algal bloom events
connections.to: Agricultural production and food supply demand
connections.rationale: Declining quality of life from blooms generates political pressure for nutrient reduction action (balancing feedback). Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.from: HELCOM Baltic Sea Action Plan nutrient targets
connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: International nutrient reduction targets drive national measures reducing nutrient loads reaching island coasts. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.from: Tourism and hospitality employment on islands
connections.to: Seasonal tourism and cultural heritage visitation
connections.rationale: Tourism employment success drives further promotion and infrastructure investment (reinforcing feedback)
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Cultural identity linked to island maritime heritage
connections.to: Traditional Baltic fishing and farming livelihoods
connections.rationale: Strong maritime cultural identity on Baltic islands sustains demand for traditional fishing and farming livelihoods
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.to: Invasive round goby altering nearshore food webs
connections.rationale: Eutrophication-altered habitats with reduced native macrophyte cover favour invasive round goby establishment. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (American mink, rats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Native seabird colony breeding success (eider, terns, guillemots)
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators depredate eggs, chicks, and nesting adults, severely reducing seabird breeding success on baltic island systems
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Invasive predator control and biosecurity programmes
connections.to: Invasive mammalian predators (American mink, rats) on native wildlife
connections.rationale: Systematic eradication and ongoing biosecurity measures remove invasive predators, allowing native wildlife recovery on islands
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
connections.rationale: Tourism and island activities generate waste volumes that exceed limited island disposal and recycling capacity, particularly during peak season

connections.from: Strandflat Fucus and filamentous algal mat extent
connections.to: Herring and perch stock condition around islands
connections.rationale: Fucus canopy provides spawning substrate and nursery habitat for coastal herring and perch; shift to filamentous algae degrades habitat quality for juvenile fish

connections.from: Grey seal management plans (population control debate)
connections.to: Small-scale coastal fishing (herring, perch, pike)
connections.rationale: Seal management measures reducing gear damage encourage fishers to maintain or resume coastal gillnet fishing operations around islands

connections.from: Coastal farmland drainage to nearshore waters
connections.to: Nutrient enrichment from agricultural sources
connections.rationale: Agricultural drainage carries dissolved nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus) from farmland into nearshore island waters.
references: HELCOM 2018 - Sources of nutrient inputs to the Baltic Sea
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Agricultural nutrient enrichment drives eutrophication and cyanobacteria blooms in shallow island waters.. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation
references: HELCOM 2018 - Eutrophication in the Baltic Sea
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Wind energy development in shallow island waters
connections.to: Recreational boating and sailing
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Wind energy development in shallow island waters

connections.to: Recreational boating and sailing
