references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Limestone quarrying produces sediment and alkalinity but is not a significant source of nitrogen or phosphorus that drive eutrophication and cyanobacteria blooms. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Offshore wind farm construction and operation
connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Offshore wind farm construction causes localized sediment disturbance but is not a meaningful source of nutrient enrichment or eutrophication driver. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.from: Winter ice reduction affecting ice-dependent species
connections.to: Coastal meadow breeding bird populations (waders, geese)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Winter ice reduction affecting ice-dependent species
references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"
Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"

connections.from: Coastal meadow breeding bird populations (waders, geese)
connections.to: Recreational and aesthetic value of island beaches
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coastal meadow breeding bird populations (waders, geese)
references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
connections.to: Recreational and aesthetic value of island beaches
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Coastal meadow biodiversity and grazing landscape heritage
connections.to: Tourism and hospitality employment on islands
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Coastal meadow biodiversity and grazing landscape heritage
references: Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Coastal meadow grazing restoration has marginal effect on large-scale eutrophication. While grazed meadows can absorb some nutrients, the effect relative to catchment-scale agricultural loading is small. Strength reduced to weak.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.from: EU MSFD Good Environmental Status indicators for islands
connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for EU MSFD Good Environmental Status indicators for islands. Nutrient loading (particularly nitrogen and phosphorus from agricultural runoff and municipal wastewater) drives phytoplankton and cyanobacterial bloom formation
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"

connections.to: Eutrophication and cyanobacteria washing ashore on island beaches
connections.rationale: Seabird nesting island access restrictions protect nesting birds from disturbance but do not address nutrient sources driving eutrophication. Different management targets. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Elmgren & Larsson (2001) "Nitrogen and the Baltic Sea"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (American mink, rats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators (mink, rats) do not directly cause coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shorelines. No scientific basis for this causal link. Strength reduced to weak, confidence to 1.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Cultural identity linked to island maritime heritage
connections.to: Military and strategic heritage value
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways

connections.to: Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
connections.rationale: Grey seal haul-out activity causes minimal, localized surface disturbance that is negligible compared to wave action and sea-level rise as drivers of coastal erosion. Strength and confidence reduced.
references: Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
Claudet & Fraschetti (2010) "Human-driven impacts on marine habitats"

connections.from: Agricultural production and food supply demand
connections.to: Limestone quarrying (Gotland, Saaremaa)
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
connections.to: Coastal erosion of limestone and glacial till shores
connections.rationale: Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure do not cause coastal erosion of shorelines. These are independent pressures. No scientific basis for causal link. Strength to weak, confidence to 1.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.from: Grey seal management plans (population control debate)
connections.to: Agricultural production and food supply demand
connections.rationale: Bridge connection to integrate isolated subgraph into main DAPSI(W)R(M) network. The positive feedback operates through direct resource dependency, where changes in the source variable proportionally affect the target variable through established ecological and socioeconomic pathways
references: Snoeijs-Leijonmalm et al. (2017) "Biological Oceanography of the Baltic Sea"
Borja et al. (2010) "Marine management towards an integrated implementation"
HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

arctic_island.description: High Arctic island systems (Svalbard, Jan Mayen, Franz Josef Land, Canadian Arctic Archipelago, Greenland settlements). Characterised by permafrost coasts, polar desert and tundra vegetation, massive seabird colonies, marine mammal haul-outs, extreme seasonality (polar night/midnight sun), sea ice dependency, and governance under Arctic Council frameworks.

drivers.name: Arctic shipping route access (Northern Sea Route)

activities.name: Arctic shipping and supply vessel traffic

pressures.name: Permafrost thaw destabilizing coastal infrastructure
Ballast water-mediated invasive species introduction
Oil spill risk in ice-covered waters
Black carbon deposition on snow and ice
Military legacy contamination (Cold War sites)
Sea ice loss reducing habitat for ice-dependent species
Invasive mammalian predators (Arctic fox, rats) on native wildlife
Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
Disturbance to seabird breeding colonies from tourism
Direct harvest mortality on marine mammals

states.name: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
Polar bear (Ursus maritimus) denning habitat availability
Permafrost coastal integrity and erosion rate
Native seabird colony breeding success (little auk, guillemots, puffins)

impacts.name: Subsistence food provisioning from marine resources
Carbon storage in permafrost soils
Seabird nutrient transport to terrestrial ecosystems
Wildlife viewing and wilderness experience

welfare.name: Indigenous community food security and cultural continuity
Expedition tourism revenue for Arctic settlements
Scientific knowledge value from Arctic monitoring
Sense of place and wilderness identity for residents

responses.name: Svalbard Environmental Protection Act enforcement
Arctic Council AMAP contaminant monitoring programme
Indigenous co-management agreements for marine resources
Strict tourism landing permits and visitor quotas
Clean-up of Cold War military waste sites
Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes

connections.to: Polar expedition cruise tourism
connections.rationale: Growing global demand for polar wilderness experiences drives increasing expedition cruise landings at Arctic island sites
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Subsistence hunting (seal, walrus, seabird)
connections.rationale: Indigenous communities depend on traditional marine mammal and seabird harvests for food security in remote Arctic settlements

connections.from: Sea ice loss reducing habitat for ice-dependent species
connections.to: Polar bear (Ursus maritimus) denning habitat availability
connections.rationale: Declining sea ice forces polar bears ashore earlier, reduces hunting platform availability, and degrades denning habitat through permafrost thaw

connections.to: Permafrost coastal integrity and erosion rate
connections.rationale: Thermal erosion of permafrost accelerates coastal retreat rates, undermining infrastructure and releasing stored organic carbon
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Permafrost coastal integrity and erosion rate
connections.to: Carbon storage in permafrost soils
connections.rationale: Intact permafrost coasts maintain vast carbon stores; erosion releases ancient organic carbon into the marine system
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.to: Seabird nutrient transport to terrestrial ecosystems
connections.rationale: Large seabird colonies transfer marine-derived nitrogen and phosphorus to terrestrial tundra via guano, supporting plant productivity
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.to: Wildlife viewing and wilderness experience
connections.rationale: Active walrus haul-outs are key attractions for expedition tourism, enhancing wildlife viewing opportunities
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Expedition tourism revenue for Arctic settlements
connections.rationale: High-quality wildlife encounters drive willingness-to-pay and repeat visitation, generating revenue for local Arctic communities
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Subsistence food provisioning from marine resources
connections.to: Indigenous community food security and cultural continuity
connections.rationale: Marine subsistence harvests are irreplaceable for food security, nutrition, and cultural identity in remote Arctic indigenous communities

connections.from: Strict tourism landing permits and visitor quotas
connections.to: Polar expedition cruise tourism
connections.rationale: Landing permits and site-specific visitor quotas limit the frequency and intensity of tourist landings at sensitive wildlife sites
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Indigenous co-management agreements for marine resources
connections.to: Subsistence hunting (seal, walrus, seabird)
connections.rationale: Co-management agreements set sustainable harvest quotas balancing indigenous rights with population conservation needs

connections.from: Expedition tourism revenue for Arctic settlements
connections.to: Expedition cruise tourism demand
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: tourism revenue incentivizes local communities and operators to promote further expedition cruise development
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Sea ice loss reducing habitat for ice-dependent species
connections.to: Arctic char (Salvelinus alpinus) populations
connections.rationale: Warming coastal waters and reduced ice cover alter prey availability and thermal habitat for cold-adapted Arctic char populations

connections.from: Indigenous community food security and cultural continuity
connections.to: Indigenous subsistence needs (hunting, fishing)
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: food security outcomes and cultural continuity reinforce community commitment to subsistence harvesting, sustaining the driver for traditional hunting and fishing practices

connections.to: Ballast water-mediated invasive species introduction
connections.rationale: Permafrost thaw does not directly cause ballast water introductions. The link (thaw enables more shipping, which increases ballast water risk) is very indirect. Confidence lowered.
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.to: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.rationale: Healthy Arctic char populations contribute to prey availability supporting seabird foraging in coastal waters
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (Arctic fox, rats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Native seabird colony breeding success (little auk, guillemots, puffins)
connections.rationale: Invasive mammalian predators depredate eggs, chicks, and nesting adults, severely reducing seabird breeding success on arctic island systems
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Invasive predator eradication and biosecurity programmes
connections.to: Invasive mammalian predators (Arctic fox, rats) on native wildlife
connections.rationale: Systematic eradication and ongoing biosecurity measures remove invasive predators, allowing native wildlife recovery on islands
references: Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.to: Limited waste disposal capacity and landfill pressure
connections.rationale: Tourism and island activities generate waste volumes that exceed limited island disposal and recycling capacity, particularly during peak season

connections.to: Disturbance to seabird breeding colonies from tourism
connections.rationale: The activity directly creates the pressure of disturbance to seabird breeding colonies from tourism
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.from: Disturbance to seabird breeding colonies from tourism
connections.to: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.rationale: Tourist landings near seabird colonies cause disturbance-induced nest abandonment, particularly in little auks and Brünnich's guillemots during incubation
references: Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Direct harvest mortality on marine mammals
connections.rationale: The activity directly creates the pressure of direct harvest mortality on marine mammals

connections.from: Direct harvest mortality on marine mammals
connections.to: Walrus (Odobenus rosmarus) haul-out site occupancy
connections.rationale: Hunting pressure and associated disturbance reduce walrus haul-out site fidelity and local population density

connections.to: Polar expedition cruise tourism
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Mineral and oil exploration interest
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

connections.to: Polar expedition cruise tourism
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Climate research and monitoring demand
references: AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"
Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Davenport and Davenport 2006 Estuarine Coastal and Shelf Science

references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.from: Arctic shipping and supply vessel traffic
connections.to: Permafrost thaw destabilizing coastal infrastructure
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Arctic shipping and supply vessel traffic
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"
Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Oil spill risk in ice-covered waters
connections.to: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.rationale: Oil spills are devastating to seabird colonies, causing direct mortality through oiling and long-term reproductive impacts. The effect is strongly negative. Polarity corrected from + to -. Well-documented (AMAP, Wiese & Robertson 2004).
references: AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"
Wassmann et al. (2011) "Footprints of climate change in the Arctic marine ecosystem"
Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.from: Black carbon deposition on snow and ice
connections.to: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.rationale: Black carbon accelerates snow and ice melt, indirectly affecting seabird prey availability (ice-associated food webs) and potentially altering nesting conditions. Effect is negative. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"
Wassmann et al. (2011) "Footprints of climate change in the Arctic marine ecosystem"
Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

connections.to: Seabird colony populations (little auk, Brünnich's guillemot)
connections.rationale: Legacy contaminants (PCBs, heavy metals) from Cold War military sites bioaccumulate in Arctic food webs and negatively affect seabird reproduction and survival (AMAP POPs assessment). Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Scientific knowledge value from Arctic monitoring
connections.to: Indigenous subsistence needs (hunting, fishing)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Scientific knowledge value from Arctic monitoring
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Svalbard Environmental Protection Act enforcement
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"
Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Arctic Council AMAP contaminant monitoring programme
connections.to: Permafrost thaw destabilizing coastal infrastructure
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Arctic Council AMAP contaminant monitoring programme
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.from: Clean-up of Cold War military waste sites
connections.to: Permafrost thaw destabilizing coastal infrastructure
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Clean-up of Cold War military waste sites
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
AMAP (2017) "Snow, Water, Ice and Permafrost in the Arctic"

connections.from: Arctic Council AMAP contaminant monitoring programme
connections.to: Arctic shipping route access (Northern Sea Route)
connections.rationale: Environmental monitoring does not directly promote shipping route access. The connection is very indirect (monitoring informs policy which may regulate shipping). Strength and confidence lowered.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Borja et al. (2010) "Marine management towards an integrated implementation"

connections.from: Invasive mammalian predators (Arctic fox, rats) on native wildlife
connections.to: Arctic char (Salvelinus alpinus) populations
connections.rationale: Invasive predators (rats, foxes) can prey on Arctic char eggs and juveniles in shallow streams, and compete for food resources. Effect is negative on char populations. Polarity corrected from + to -.
references: Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"
FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Wassmann et al. 2011 Progress in Oceanography

atlantic_estuary.description: Major Atlantic European estuaries (Tagus, Loire, Severn, Elbe, Gironde, Shannon). Large tidal-range transitional waters with turbidity maximum zones, extensive mudflats and saltmarshes, critical migratory fish habitat, legacy industrial contamination, and major port infrastructure.

drivers.name: Flood risk management for estuarine communities

activities.name: Commercial port operations and maintenance dredging

pressures.name: Chronic chemical contamination (heavy metals, PCBs)
Altered flow regime from upstream dams and abstraction
Habitat loss from land claim and channelization
Microplastic accumulation in estuarine sediments
Thermal plumes from coastal power stations
Altered freshwater flow regime from upstream regulation

states.name: Benthic invertebrate diversity in estuarine mud
Turbidity maximum zone dynamics and position
Estuarine salinity gradient and mixing zone dynamics
Chinese mitten crab and Pacific oyster (Eriocheir sinensis, Magallana gigas) invasion

impacts.name: Migratory fish provisioning (eel, shad, lamprey)
Saltmarsh coastal flood attenuation
Nursery habitat for marine and diadromous species
Carbon sequestration in saltmarsh sediments

welfare.name: Artisanal fishing community livelihoods
Port-related employment and economic output
Flood damage avoidance for estuarine settlements
Waterfront amenity and recreational value

responses.name: EU Water Framework Directive ecological status measures
Fish passage restoration (weir and barrier removal)
Contaminated sediment dredging and disposal protocols
Managed realignment for flood defence and habitat creation
OSPAR/ICES eel management plans

connections.to: Commercial port operations and maintenance dredging
connections.rationale: Growing trade volumes and vessel sizes drive continuous dredging to maintain and deepen navigation channels

connections.from: Commercial port operations and maintenance dredging
connections.to: Chronic chemical contamination (heavy metals, PCBs)
connections.rationale: Dredging resuspends legacy contaminants bound to fine estuarine sediments, remobilizing heavy metals and PCBs into the water column

connections.to: Industrial and urban wastewater discharge
connections.rationale: Agricultural intensification in the catchment increases diffuse nutrient and pesticide loading into watercourses that discharge to the estuary

connections.from: Altered flow regime from upstream dams and abstraction
connections.to: Allis shad (Alosa alosa) migration success
connections.rationale: Dams block upstream spawning migration of allis shad and altered flows disrupt migration cues essential for this anadromous species

connections.to: European eel (Anguilla anguilla) recruitment
connections.rationale: Bioaccumulation of PCBs and heavy metals in eel fat reserves impairs reproduction and reduces silver eel escapement fitness, contributing to recruitment collapse

connections.from: Habitat loss from land claim and channelization
connections.to: Saltmarsh extent and condition
connections.rationale: Historical land claim and ongoing channelization have eliminated >50% of Atlantic estuarine saltmarsh, with continuing losses from coastal squeeze

connections.to: Saltmarsh coastal flood attenuation
connections.rationale: Extensive saltmarshes attenuate wave energy and store tidal floodwater, providing measurable flood risk reduction for hinterland areas

connections.to: Flood damage avoidance for estuarine settlements
connections.rationale: Natural flood attenuation by saltmarshes reduces economic damages and insurance costs for adjacent estuarine communities

connections.to: Migratory fish provisioning (eel, shad, lamprey)
connections.rationale: Eel recruitment levels directly determine the biomass available for traditional estuarine eel fisheries. Fishing mortality above sustainable levels reduces spawning stock biomass, lowering reproductive output and population resilience

connections.to: Artisanal fishing community livelihoods
connections.rationale: Artisanal eel and shad fisheries have sustained estuarine communities for centuries; declining catches directly threaten livelihoods

connections.from: Fish passage restoration (weir and barrier removal)
connections.to: Altered flow regime from upstream dams and abstraction
connections.rationale: Weir removal restores more natural flow regimes and re-opens migration corridors for diadromous fish species

connections.from: Flood damage avoidance for estuarine settlements
connections.to: Flood risk management for estuarine communities
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: experienced or anticipated flood damage reinforces political and public demand for flood risk management investment
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Migratory fish exploitation (shad, eel, lamprey)
connections.rationale: EU eel regulation and national management plans restrict fishing effort, seasonal closures, and glass eel catch quotas across Atlantic estuaries

connections.to: Port and shipping expansion demand
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: port employment and economic benefits create political and commercial momentum for further port expansion and shipping infrastructure investment

connections.from: Flood risk management for estuarine communities
connections.to: Tidal energy project development
connections.rationale: Flood risk awareness drives investment in tidal barrage infrastructure that combines flood management with energy generation
references: Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.to: Chinese mitten crab and Pacific oyster (Eriocheir sinensis, Magallana gigas) invasion
connections.rationale: Contamination weakens native species competitively, facilitating establishment of pollution-tolerant invasive species. Marine invasive species alter ecosystem structure and function through competition, predation, and habitat modification

connections.to: Benthic invertebrate diversity in estuarine mud
connections.rationale: Healthy saltmarsh provides detrital inputs and sheltered habitat supporting diverse benthic invertebrate communities

connections.to: European eel (Anguilla anguilla) recruitment
connections.rationale: Saltmarsh creeks provide critical feeding and growth habitat for glass eels during estuarine recruitment; saltmarsh loss reduces available nursery habitat and eel recruitment success

connections.from: Managed realignment for flood defence and habitat creation
connections.to: Habitat loss from land claim and channelization
connections.rationale: Managed realignment counteracts habitat loss from historical land claim by breaching sea walls and allowing saltmarsh to develop

connections.to: Commercial port operations and maintenance dredging
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Urban and industrial water demand
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Commercial port operations and maintenance dredging
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Renewable energy interest (tidal power)
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Chronic chemical contamination (heavy metals, PCBs)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Recreational sailing and waterfront development
references: Ronnback et al. (2007) "Ecosystem goods and services from Swedish coastal habitats"

connections.to: Chronic chemical contamination (heavy metals, PCBs)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Aggregate extraction from estuary bed
references: Borja et al. (2010) "Marine management towards an integrated implementation"

connections.to: European eel (Anguilla anguilla) recruitment
connections.rationale: Microplastic ingestion by recruiting glass eels causes gut inflammation and reduced feeding efficiency, negatively affecting recruitment success.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Halpern et al. (2015) "Spatial and temporal changes in cumulative human impacts"

connections.from: Thermal plumes from coastal power stations
connections.to: European eel (Anguilla anguilla) recruitment
connections.rationale: Thermal plumes create temperature barriers that disrupt glass eel migration, and cooling water entrainment causes direct mortality during recruitment.
references: Halpern et al. (2008) "A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems"
Poloczanska et al. 2013 Nature Climate Change

connections.from: Altered freshwater flow regime from upstream regulation
connections.to: European eel (Anguilla anguilla) recruitment
connections.rationale: Flow regulation disrupts migration cues and creates physical barriers to glass eel passage, reducing recruitment success (ICES WGEEL reports).
references: Borja et al. (2010) "Marine management towards an integrated implementation"
Folke et al. 2005 Annual Review of Environment and Resources

connections.from: Turbidity maximum zone dynamics and position
connections.to: Migratory fish provisioning (eel, shad, lamprey)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Turbidity maximum zone dynamics and position
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Estuarine salinity gradient and mixing zone dynamics
connections.to: Migratory fish provisioning (eel, shad, lamprey)
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Estuarine salinity gradient and mixing zone dynamics
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"

connections.from: Nursery habitat for marine and diadromous species
connections.to: Artisanal fishing community livelihoods
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Nursery habitat for marine and diadromous species
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Artisanal fishing community livelihoods
connections.rationale: Connection added to complete DAPSI(W)R(M) chain for Carbon sequestration in saltmarsh sediments
references: FAO (2020) "The State of World Fisheries and Aquaculture"
Worm et al. (2006) "Impacts of biodiversity loss on ocean ecosystem services"

connections.to: Port and shipping expansion demand

atlantic_open_coast.description: Northeast Atlantic exposed coastlines (Portuguese coast, Galician rias, Bay of Biscay, Celtic Sea coast, Norwegian coast). High-energy wave environments with kelp forests, rocky reefs, sandy beaches, strong fishing traditions, emerging offshore renewable energy, and Atlantic gyre plastic pollution exposure.

baltic_rocky_coast.description: Baltic rocky archipelago coasts - Schären (German) / Skärgård (Swedish). Exposed bedrock shores of the Swedish east coast (Stockholm to Öregrund), Finnish Turku archipelago outer zone, Åland rocky shores, and Estonian north coast. Low-salinity (5-7 PSU) environment where Fucus vesiculosus grows only to ~6m depth (vs 15m+ in Atlantic), Mytilus trossulus (NOT M. edulis) dominates rocky substrates, and sea trout (Salmo trutta) is the primary coastal salmonid.

drivers.name: Recreational boating and summer cottage culture
Coastal erosion awareness and shore protection demand
Scientific research interest in Baltic rocky communities

activities.name: Shore-based recreational fishing (spinning for sea trout, trolling for pike)
Blue mussel (Mytilus trossulus) harvesting for feed/nutrient removal
Research monitoring of rocky shore algal communities
Kayaking and sailing tourism in archipelago

pressures.name: Antifouling paint contamination (TBT legacy, copper/zinc current)
Wave erosion from recreational boat wakes
Cyanobacteria accumulation on rocky shores in summer
Oil spill exposure from Baltic shipping lanes
Eutrophication-driven filamentous algae overgrowth

states.name: Fucus vesiculosus (bladderwrack) depth distribution and coverage
Mytilus trossulus (Baltic blue mussel) density on rocky substrate
Grey seal predation on coastal fish stocks and gear damage
Seagrass (Zostera marina) patches in brackish sheltered bays along rocky coast

impacts.name: Fucus belt habitat provisioning for juvenile fish and invertebrates
Mussel-mediated water filtration and nutrient cycling
Recreational fishing quality for sea trout anglers
Archipelago landscape aesthetic and recreational value

welfare.name: Sea trout recreational fishing tourism revenue
Summer cottage and archipelago property values
Sense of place and cultural identity for coastal communities
Health and well-being from archipelago recreation

responses.name: HELCOM Baltic Sea Action Plan eutrophication measures
Antifouling paint regulations (EU Biocidal Products Regulation)
Natura 2000 reef habitat (1170) protection for rocky shores
Boat speed restrictions near sensitive shores and bird colonies
National eutrophication reduction targets (nitrogen, phosphorus)

connections.from: Recreational boating and summer cottage culture
connections.to: Recreational boating and marina operations
connections.rationale: Strong Nordic summer cottage and boating traditions drive intensive recreational vessel traffic and marina development along archipelago coasts
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Antifouling paint contamination (TBT legacy, copper/zinc current)
connections.rationale: Dense recreational fleets release copper and zinc from modern antifouling paints, with legacy TBT persisting in marina sediments from pre-ban coatings

connections.to: Wave erosion from recreational boat wakes
connections.rationale: Wake waves from motorboats erode soft sediment pockets between bedrock and disturb shallow Fucus belt habitats in narrow archipelago channels

connections.to: Fucus vesiculosus (bladderwrack) depth distribution and coverage
connections.rationale: Excess nutrients promote epiphytic filamentous algae that smother Fucus fronds, reducing photosynthesis and restricting Fucus to <6m depth in the low-salinity Baltic
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Filamentous algae (Cladophora, Pilayella) cover ratio
connections.rationale: Nutrient enrichment directly stimulates Cladophora and Pilayella growth, which can form thick mats covering rocky substrate and displacing perennial macroalgae
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Fucus vesiculosus (bladderwrack) depth distribution and coverage
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: filamentous algae overgrowth shades and smothers Fucus, reducing canopy which further opens space for filamentous colonization

connections.from: Fucus vesiculosus (bladderwrack) depth distribution and coverage
connections.to: Fucus belt habitat provisioning for juvenile fish and invertebrates
connections.rationale: The Fucus belt is the primary structural habitat on Baltic rocky coasts, supporting 100+ invertebrate species and serving as nursery for juvenile perch, pike, and sea trout

connections.from: Mytilus trossulus (Baltic blue mussel) density on rocky substrate
connections.to: Mussel-mediated water filtration and nutrient cycling
connections.rationale: Baltic blue mussels (M. trossulus, smaller than Atlantic M. edulis due to low salinity stress) filter phytoplankton and transfer nutrients from water column to benthos
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Grey seal predation on coastal fish stocks and gear damage
connections.to: Sea trout (Salmo trutta) coastal populations
connections.rationale: Recovering grey seal populations prey on coastal sea trout and damage fishing gear, creating conflict with recreational and small-scale fisheries

connections.to: Recreational fishing quality for sea trout anglers
connections.rationale: Sea trout abundance directly determines catch rates and trophy potential for shore-based spinning anglers on Baltic rocky coasts

connections.from: Recreational fishing quality for sea trout anglers
connections.to: Sea trout recreational fishing tourism revenue
connections.rationale: High-quality sea trout fishing attracts dedicated anglers who generate accommodation, guide, and equipment spending in coastal communities

connections.from: HELCOM Baltic Sea Action Plan eutrophication measures
connections.to: Eutrophication-driven filamentous algae overgrowth
connections.rationale: BSAP nutrient reduction targets, if met, would reduce the nutrient loading that drives filamentous algae blooms on rocky shores
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Antifouling paint regulations (EU Biocidal Products Regulation)
connections.to: Antifouling paint contamination (TBT legacy, copper/zinc current)
connections.rationale: EU BPR restrictions on biocidal antifouling substances reduce copper/zinc input from recreational vessels, though TBT legacy persists in sediments

connections.from: Archipelago landscape aesthetic and recreational value
connections.to: Summer cottage and archipelago property values
connections.rationale: Clear water, healthy rocky shore communities, and attractive archipelago landscapes drive property demand and values along the coast
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Summer cottage and archipelago property values
connections.to: Recreational boating and summer cottage culture
connections.rationale: Feedback loop: high property values and amenity attract further investment in summer cottages and boating infrastructure, reinforcing the recreational demand driver
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.to: Shore-based recreational fishing (spinning for sea trout, trolling for pike)
connections.rationale: Strong fishing tradition directly sustains shore-based recreational fishing participation on Baltic rocky coasts
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Oil spill exposure from Baltic shipping lanes
connections.to: Cyanobacteria accumulation on rocky shores in summer
connections.rationale: Oil spill exposure and cyanobacteria accumulation are largely independent pressures. Oil may kill some grazers allowing cyanobacteria, but evidence for this specific pathway in Baltic rocky shores is limited. Confidence reduced from 4 to 2.
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Fucus vesiculosus (bladderwrack) depth distribution and coverage
connections.to: Mytilus trossulus (Baltic blue mussel) density on rocky substrate
connections.rationale: Bladderwrack canopy provides sheltered microhabitat and detrital food that supports blue mussel settlement and survival on rocky shores
references: HELCOM 2018 State of the Baltic Sea

connections.from: Antifouling paint regulations (EU Biocidal Products Regulation)
connections.to: Recreational boating and marina operations
connections.rationale: Stricter antifouling regulations increase maintenance costs and reduce toxic leaching, modifying boating practices and hull cleaning frequency near sensitive rocky shore habitats

macaronesia_island.description: Volcanic island coastal ecosystems of the Macaronesian archipelagos (Azores, Madeira, Canary Islands, Cape Verde). Characterized by steep rocky shorelines, Cystoseira and Sargassum macroalgal forests, high endemism, and cetacean-rich waters subject to intense tourism and artisanal fishing pressure.

drivers.name: Cultural heritage of island fishing traditions

pressures.name: Marine litter accumulation on island coasts
Overfishing of demersal fish stocks
Ocean warming and marine heatwaves
Invasive species introduction (e.g., Caulerpa, Asparagopsis)
Habitat degradation from boat anchoring
Brine discharge from desalination
Underwater noise from vessel traffic
Coastal urbanization and land reclamation

states.name: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
Rocky reef community structure and endemism

impacts.name: Reduced artisanal fish catches
Loss of endemic marine species
Degraded diving and snorkeling site quality
Altered nearshore food web structure
Decline in coastal carbon sequestration

welfare.name: Fishing community livelihoods and income
Marine tourism revenue
Cultural identity of island fishing communities
Local food security from seafood
Recreational wellbeing from ocean access
Coastal property values

responses.name: Azores Marine Park and MPA network expansion
Fishing effort and quota regulations
Marine spatial planning for Macaronesian waters
Invasive species monitoring and removal programmes
EU Biodiversity Strategy implementation
Sustainable tourism certification schemes
Marine litter reduction action plans
OSPAR Convention measures for NE Atlantic

connections.to: Recreational diving and whale watching
connections.rationale: Growing international tourism demand drives expansion of whale watching in the Azores and diving tourism in the Canary Islands, making marine recreation the primary economic activity for many coastal communities across Macaronesia.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Port and marina development
connections.rationale: Increasing tourist arrivals to the Canary Islands and Azores drive demand for new marina berths and port infrastructure expansions, particularly for recreational boating and cruise ship operations.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Artisanal demersal fishing
connections.rationale: CFP regulations shape artisanal fishing effort across Macaronesia, with specific provisions for outermost regions (Azores, Madeira, Canaries) that determine fleet size, gear restrictions, and total allowable catches for demersal species.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Ocean warming and marine heatwaves
connections.rationale: Macaronesian waters are experiencing accelerating sea surface temperature rise, with marine heatwaves becoming more frequent around the Canary Islands and Azores, shifting isotherms and altering species distributions at the subtropical-temperate boundary.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Artisanal demersal fishing
connections.rationale: Rising global and regional demand for fresh seafood increases fishing pressure on Macaronesian demersal stocks, particularly high-value species like black scabbardfish (Aphanopus carbo) and wreckfish exported from the Azores and Madeira.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Desalination plant operation
connections.rationale: Growing resident and tourist populations on water-scarce Canary Islands and Cape Verde drive rapid expansion of desalination capacity, with the Canaries operating over 300 desalination plants to meet freshwater demand.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Coastal urbanization and land reclamation
connections.rationale: Population pressure on the limited coastal plains of volcanic Macaronesian islands, particularly in Gran Canaria and Tenerife, drives coastal construction and land reclamation that destroys intertidal habitats.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Underwater noise from vessel traffic
connections.rationale: Intensive whale-watching operations in the Azores and southern Tenerife generate chronic underwater noise from repeated vessel approaches to cetacean groups, potentially causing behavioral disruption and habitat displacement.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Habitat degradation from boat anchoring
connections.rationale: Anchor damage from recreational boats in the Canary Islands and Madeira physically destroys Cystoseira forests and rocky reef communities, with studies documenting significant macroalgal canopy loss in popular anchorage sites.
references: Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems

connections.to: Overfishing of demersal fish stocks
connections.rationale: Artisanal fishing fleets across the Azores, Madeira, and Canaries exert significant cumulative pressure on slow-growing demersal species like blackspot seabream (Pagellus bogaraveo) and black scabbardfish, which have limited recovery potential.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Brine discharge from desalination
connections.rationale: Desalination plants in the Canary Islands and Cape Verde discharge hypersaline brine into nearshore waters, locally increasing salinity and temperature, which can stress benthic communities on the volcanic rocky substrate.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Marine litter accumulation on island coasts
connections.rationale: Lost and discarded fishing gear from artisanal fleets contributes to marine litter on Macaronesian coastlines, with beach surveys documenting fishing-related debris as a major litter category across all archipelagos.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.rationale: Marine heatwaves in the Canary Islands cause bleaching and mortality of Cystoseira abies-marina and other canopy-forming macroalgae, with documented declines across the eastern Canary Islands correlating with rising sea surface temperatures.
references: Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Wirtz et al. (2013) The invasive species of Macaronesia

connections.to: Cetacean population abundance and diversity
connections.rationale: Warming waters around the Azores alter prey distribution and migration patterns for resident cetacean populations including sperm whales and bottlenose dolphins, potentially reducing habitat suitability in traditionally productive areas.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.rationale: Invasive macroalgae such as Asparagopsis taxiformis and Caulerpa cylindracea compete with native Cystoseira and Sargassum forests in the Canary Islands and Madeira, altering community composition and reducing native canopy cover.
references: Wirtz et al. (2013) The invasive species of Macaronesia
Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region

connections.to: Demersal fish stock biomass
connections.rationale: Chronic overfishing of slow-growing demersal species in Macaronesian waters has depleted stocks of blackspot seabream, wreckfish, and red porgy, with stock assessments indicating biomass levels below sustainable reference points.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.rationale: Mechanical damage from anchor chains on shallow rocky reefs in the Canary Islands and Madeira fragments and uproots Cystoseira canopies, with recovery taking years to decades on volcanic substrate.
references: Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region

connections.to: Cetacean population abundance and diversity
connections.rationale: Chronic underwater noise from whale-watching vessels and shipping around the Azores and southern Tenerife causes behavioral changes in sperm whales and Risso's dolphins, including reduced foraging time and habitat avoidance.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Marine litter accumulation on island coasts
connections.to: Rocky reef community structure and endemism
connections.rationale: Marine litter, including microplastics, accumulates in Macaronesian coastal waters and sediments, entering food webs through ingestion by endemic invertebrates and fish species on volcanic rocky reefs.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.to: Degraded diving and snorkeling site quality
connections.rationale: Decline of Cystoseira forests reduces the structural complexity and biodiversity of Canary Island dive sites, diminishing the aesthetic and ecological value that attracts recreational divers to Macaronesian waters.
references: Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region

connections.to: Reduced artisanal fish catches
connections.rationale: Declining demersal fish biomass in Azores, Madeira, and Canary Islands waters directly reduces catch per unit effort for artisanal fishers targeting blackspot seabream, parrotfish, and other reef-associated species.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.from: Rocky reef community structure and endemism
connections.to: Loss of endemic marine species
connections.rationale: Degradation of rocky reef communities threatens Macaronesian endemic species, including the Azores chromis (Chromis limbata) and numerous endemic invertebrates found only on volcanic island reefs.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Wirtz et al. (2013) The invasive species of Macaronesia

connections.from: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.to: Altered nearshore food web structure
connections.rationale: Loss of Cystoseira canopy in the Canary Islands removes critical nursery habitat and trophic base for nearshore fish and invertebrate communities, cascading through the coastal food web.
references: Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands

connections.to: Degraded diving and snorkeling site quality
connections.rationale: Declining cetacean populations in Azores and Canary Islands waters reduce the quality and reliability of whale-watching and swim-with-dolphin experiences that form a major tourist attraction.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Fishing community livelihoods and income
connections.rationale: Declining catches directly reduce income for artisanal fishing households across all Macaronesian archipelagos, where fishing remains a primary livelihood for many coastal communities with limited alternative employment.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.from: Degraded diving and snorkeling site quality
connections.to: Marine tourism revenue
connections.rationale: Reduced quality of diving sites and whale-watching encounters in the Canary Islands and Azores threatens the marine tourism sector that generates hundreds of millions of euros annually for island economies.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Local food security from seafood
connections.rationale: Declining local fish landings reduce the availability and increase the price of fresh seafood in Macaronesian island markets, affecting food security in communities historically dependent on locally caught fish.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Cultural identity of island fishing communities
connections.rationale: Loss of endemic species and traditional target fish erodes the cultural connection between Macaronesian island communities and their marine environment, diminishing traditions of knowledge and identity built around specific species.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment

connections.to: Fishing effort and quota regulations
connections.rationale: Declining fisher incomes across Macaronesian islands create political pressure for both quota adjustments and socioeconomic support measures, driving policy responses at regional and EU levels.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Sustainable tourism certification schemes
connections.rationale: The economic importance of marine tourism to Macaronesian island economies motivates development of sustainability certifications for whale watching, diving operators, and coastal hotels to protect the resource base.
references: Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.from: Azores Marine Park and MPA network expansion
connections.to: Overfishing of demersal fish stocks
connections.rationale: The Azores Marine Park network, including no-take reserves at Formigas and Dollabarat, reduces fishing pressure on demersal stocks within protected areas, allowing stock recovery and spillover to adjacent fishing grounds.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment

connections.from: Invasive species monitoring and removal programmes
connections.to: Invasive species introduction (e.g., Caulerpa, Asparagopsis)
connections.rationale: Monitoring and early detection programmes in the Canary Islands and Azores aim to control invasive macroalgae and fish species before establishment, though eradication success remains limited for established populations.
references: Wirtz et al. (2013) The invasive species of Macaronesia
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Marine litter accumulation on island coasts
connections.rationale: Coordinated marine litter action plans across Macaronesian archipelagos, including beach cleanups and fishing gear recycling programmes, reduce litter inputs though oceanic transport of debris from distant sources remains uncontrolled.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Marine spatial planning for Macaronesian waters
connections.to: Habitat degradation from boat anchoring
connections.rationale: Marine spatial planning in Macaronesian waters designates mooring zones and anchoring restrictions near sensitive Cystoseira habitats, reducing physical damage to benthic communities from unregulated anchoring.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems

connections.to: Demersal fish stock biomass
connections.rationale: Quota restrictions and effort controls for Macaronesian demersal fisheries allow partial stock recovery, though effectiveness is limited by enforcement challenges across remote island waters.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Coastal water quality index
connections.rationale: Fish farm effluents from sea bass and sea bream cage culture in the Canary Islands and Madeira release nutrients and organic matter that reduce water quality in the oligotrophic waters surrounding volcanic islands.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Decline in coastal carbon sequestration
connections.rationale: Degraded water quality in nearshore Macaronesian waters can reduce productivity of macroalgal forests and seagrass patches that contribute to coastal blue carbon sequestration on volcanic island shelves.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Sangil et al. (2012) Decline of Cystoseira forests in the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Port and marina development
connections.rationale: EU and national renewable ocean energy targets drive port and marina infrastructure development to support offshore wind servicing operations in Macaronesian waters, particularly around the Canary Islands and Azores.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.from: Cultural heritage of island fishing traditions
connections.to: Artisanal demersal fishing
connections.rationale: Deep cultural identity tied to fishing traditions in Macaronesian island communities sustains artisanal demersal fishing practices across generations, preserving traditional techniques and local knowledge.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Azores Marine Park and MPA network expansion
connections.rationale: The EU Biodiversity Strategy 2030 mandates 30% marine area protection, directly driving MPA expansion such as the Azores Marine Park and broader MPA network development in Macaronesian waters.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Rocky reef community structure and endemism
connections.rationale: Scientific research expeditions document and monitor endemic rocky reef species in Macaronesian waters, informing conservation priorities for these unique high-endemism assemblages.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Cystoseira and Sargassum forest extent and health
connections.rationale: Installation of submarine cables and pipelines across the Macaronesian seabed causes localised physical disturbance to shallow benthic habitats, including Cystoseira and Sargassum forests important for endemic biodiversity.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Sea surface temperature regime
connections.rationale: Increasing frequency of marine heatwaves driven by climate change is altering the sea surface temperature regime around Macaronesian islands, stressing endemic cold-water species.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Recreational wellbeing from ocean access
connections.rationale: Macaronesian island economies provide extensive marine recreation activities including diving and whale watching; direct ocean access through these activities underpins recreational wellbeing for residents and visitors.
references: Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Coastal property values
connections.rationale: Increasing ocean warming and marine heatwave frequency in Macaronesian island waters amplify coastal erosion and flood risks, progressively reducing the security and market value of coastal properties.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Azores Marine Park and MPA network expansion
connections.rationale: Implementation of EU Biodiversity Strategy commitments triggers concrete MPA designation processes in Macaronesian Exclusive Economic Zones, accelerating the Azores Marine Park and MPA network expansion.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.from: OSPAR Convention measures for NE Atlantic
connections.to: Fishing effort and quota regulations
connections.rationale: OSPAR Convention commitments for the Northeast Atlantic apply to Macaronesian waters and support coordinated fishing effort management and quota regulations to protect vulnerable marine ecosystems.
references: OSPAR (2010) "Quality Status Report for the North-East Atlantic"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

macaronesia_seamount.description: Deep-sea seamount ecosystems of Macaronesia, including the Great Meteor Seamount, Josephine Bank, and numerous unnamed seamounts surrounding the Azores. These elevated underwater volcanic features host cold-water coral gardens, deep-sea sponge aggregations, and hydrothermal vent communities with exceptionally high endemism.

Scientific research interest in deep-sea biodiversity
Renewable energy and mineral exploration targets

activities.name: Deep-water bottom trawling on seamount flanks
Deep-water longline fishing for black scabbardfish

Ocean acidification effects on deep corals

states.name: Cold-water coral garden coverage on seamount flanks

Reduced deep-water fishery productivity

Disrupted deep-sea nutrient cycling

Scientific knowledge and discovery value
Existence value of unique deep-sea ecosystems
Biotechnology potential from vent organisms
Intergenerational equity in deep-sea resources
