EU deep-sea access regulation (bottom trawl ban)
Azores deep-sea MPA designations
VME encounter protocols for fishing vessels
Deep-sea research and monitoring programmes
ISA regulations for deep-sea mining
NEAFC bottom fishing closures

connections.from: Global seafood demand for deep-water species
connections.to: Deep-water bottom trawling on seamount flanks
connections.rationale: International demand for orange roughy, alfonsinos, and other deep-water species historically drove expansion of bottom trawling on Macaronesian seamounts, particularly around the Azores and Madeira.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: Global seafood demand for deep-water species
connections.to: Deep-water longline fishing for black scabbardfish
connections.rationale: Market demand for black scabbardfish (Aphanopus carbo), a Macaronesian specialty, drives longline fishing operations from Madeira and Azores ports targeting seamount-associated aggregations.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands

connections.from: Scientific research interest in deep-sea biodiversity
connections.to: Scientific ROV surveys and sampling
connections.rationale: The exceptional biodiversity and endemism of Azores seamounts and hydrothermal vents attracts international research expeditions, with the Azores recognized as a deep-sea biodiversity hotspot in the NE Atlantic.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Deep-water bottom trawling on seamount flanks
connections.rationale: EU Regulation 2016/2336 restricting deep-sea bottom trawling below 800m has significantly curtailed trawl effort on Macaronesian seamounts, protecting vulnerable marine ecosystems from physical destruction.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Deep-ocean warming and deoxygenation
connections.rationale: Climate-driven warming of intermediate and deep water masses in the NE Atlantic is gradually altering temperature and oxygen conditions around Macaronesian seamounts, threatening temperature-sensitive cold-water coral species.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Deep-water bottom trawling on seamount flanks
connections.to: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.rationale: Bottom trawling on Macaronesian seamounts causes catastrophic and essentially irreversible destruction of cold-water corals, sponge gardens, and other sessile fauna that take centuries to millennia to grow on volcanic substrate.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Deep-water bottom trawling on seamount flanks
connections.to: Sedimentation from bottom-contact gear
connections.rationale: Trawl gear resuspends fine volcanic sediments on Macaronesian seamount flanks, creating turbidity plumes that smother filter-feeding corals and sponges across wide areas downstream of trawl tracks.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Deep-water longline fishing for black scabbardfish
connections.to: Overfishing of seamount-aggregating species
connections.rationale: Longline fishing targeting black scabbardfish aggregations over Macaronesian seamounts can deplete local populations rapidly due to the species' slow growth and the predictable nature of seamount aggregations.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.to: Overfishing of seamount-aggregating species
connections.rationale: Pelagic fishing targeting tuna and other migratory species over Macaronesian seamounts exploits the aggregation effect that concentrates prey and predators around topographic features.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.from: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.to: Cold-water coral garden coverage on seamount flanks
connections.rationale: Trawling has devastated cold-water coral gardens on previously fished Macaronesian seamounts, with ROV surveys showing near-complete removal of Lophelia pertusa and Madrepora oculata frameworks on trawled summits.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores

connections.from: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.to: Deep-sea sponge aggregation integrity
connections.rationale: Bottom trawling removes or fragments large structure-forming sponges on Macaronesian seamounts that may be hundreds of years old, with virtually no recovery potential on human timescales.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Deep-sea sponge aggregation integrity
connections.rationale: Resuspended sediment from trawling smothers filter-feeding sponge communities on Azores seamounts, clogging their aquiferous systems and reducing their ability to process food particles.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Seamount-associated fish biomass
connections.rationale: Serial depletion of fish stocks on Macaronesian seamounts, documented for orange roughy and alfonsinos, has reduced biomass to fractions of pre-exploitation levels with slow recovery due to species longevity.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.to: Cold-water coral garden coverage on seamount flanks
connections.rationale: Warming and shoaling of the aragonite saturation horizon in the NE Atlantic threatens cold-water coral survival on Macaronesian seamounts, as reduced carbonate saturation weakens coral skeletal integrity.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Ocean acidification effects on deep corals
connections.to: Cold-water coral garden coverage on seamount flanks
connections.rationale: Ocean acidification reduces calcification rates in cold-water corals on Azores seamounts, with projected aragonite undersaturation threatening Lophelia and Madrepora communities at their current depth ranges.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Cold-water coral garden coverage on seamount flanks
connections.to: Loss of vulnerable marine ecosystem structure
connections.rationale: Loss of cold-water coral frameworks on Macaronesian seamounts eliminates critical three-dimensional habitat that supports hundreds of associated species, representing irreversible loss of vulnerable marine ecosystems.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores

connections.to: Reduced deep-water fishery productivity
connections.rationale: Depleted seamount fish stocks directly reduce the productivity and economic viability of deep-water fishing operations from Azores and Madeira ports, leading to fleet contraction.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.to: Loss of undiscovered genetic resources
connections.rationale: Destruction of diverse benthic communities on Macaronesian seamounts eliminates potentially valuable genetic resources before they can be catalogued, given that an estimated 80% of seamount species remain undescribed.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Disrupted deep-sea nutrient cycling
connections.rationale: Hydrothermal vents on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge near the Azores support chemosynthetic communities that drive local nutrient cycling; disruption of these communities alters energy and material flows in the deep-sea ecosystem.
references: Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: Loss of vulnerable marine ecosystem structure
connections.to: Existence value of unique deep-sea ecosystems
connections.rationale: Irreversible destruction of unique Macaronesian seamount habitats diminishes their existence value for society, as these ecosystems are recognized as globally significant and irreplaceable natural heritage.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Deep-water fishing fleet viability
connections.rationale: Declining productivity of deep-water fisheries around Macaronesian seamounts threatens the economic viability of specialized fishing fleets based in the Azores and Madeira.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.to: Biotechnology potential from vent organisms
connections.rationale: Destruction of Azores hydrothermal vent and seamount communities eliminates potential biotechnology resources, as extremophile organisms from these habitats produce unique enzymes and bioactive compounds of pharmaceutical interest.
references: Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.from: Loss of vulnerable marine ecosystem structure
connections.to: Intergenerational equity in deep-sea resources
connections.rationale: Irreversible destruction of Macaronesian seamount ecosystems that took millennia to develop fundamentally compromises future generations' access to these natural resources and the services they provide.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Existence value of unique deep-sea ecosystems
connections.to: OSPAR seamount and hydrothermal vent protection
connections.rationale: Growing public awareness of the unique value of Macaronesian deep-sea ecosystems has driven OSPAR to designate seamounts and hydrothermal vents as protected features requiring conservation measures.
references: Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: EU deep-sea access regulation (bottom trawl ban)
connections.rationale: Economic decline of Macaronesian deep-water fleets, combined with environmental concerns, provided impetus for EU deep-sea access regulations restricting destructive fishing practices on seamounts.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: OSPAR seamount and hydrothermal vent protection
connections.to: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.rationale: OSPAR protection measures for Macaronesian seamounts, including listing of seamount and hydrothermal vent habitats as threatened and/or declining, have contributed to fishing closures that reduce physical destruction of benthic communities.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: EU deep-sea access regulation (bottom trawl ban)
connections.to: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.rationale: The EU bottom trawling depth restriction has been the single most effective measure reducing physical destruction of deep-sea habitats on Macaronesian seamounts, essentially eliminating trawl impacts below 800m.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Overfishing of seamount-aggregating species
connections.rationale: Designation of deep-sea MPAs around Azores seamounts restricts fishing activity within protected areas, reducing exploitation pressure on seamount-aggregating fish populations.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: VME encounter protocols for fishing vessels
connections.to: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.rationale: VME encounter protocols require fishing vessels to move away when catching indicator species such as cold-water corals on Macaronesian seamounts, providing real-time protection for previously unknown vulnerable habitats.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Physical destruction of seamount benthos by trawling
connections.rationale: NEAFC bottom fishing closures on international waters around Macaronesian seamounts complement EU and national measures, providing protection for seamounts beyond national jurisdiction.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Hydrothermal vent community health (Azores)
connections.rationale: Ongoing research programmes at Azores hydrothermal vent sites (Lucky Strike, Menez Gwen, Rainbow) improve understanding and inform adaptive management, though research activities themselves can cause minor physical disturbance.
references: Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Deep-sea research and monitoring programmes
connections.rationale: The high scientific value of Macaronesian seamount and vent ecosystems attracts research funding and drives establishment of long-term monitoring programmes, particularly at Azores hydrothermal vent sites.
references: Cardigos et al. (2005) Hydrothermal vent habitats of the Azores
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores

connections.to: Marine debris accumulation on seamount summits
connections.rationale: Submarine cable installation across Macaronesian seamount areas can disturb summit habitats and abandoned cables contribute to marine debris, though this impact is localized compared to trawling.
references: Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Azores deep-sea MPA designations
connections.rationale: International commitments under OSPAR and the Convention on Biological Diversity drive Portugal and Spain to designate deep-sea MPAs around Macaronesian seamounts, meeting targets for protecting vulnerable marine ecosystems.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: Marine debris accumulation on seamount summits
connections.to: Deep-sea benthic invertebrate diversity
connections.rationale: Marine debris including abandoned fishing gear and plastic waste accumulates on Macaronesian seamount summits, entangling deep-sea corals and sponges and introducing microplastics into the benthic food web.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Martins et al. (2021) Deep-sea ecosystems of the Azores

connections.from: Renewable energy and mineral exploration targets
connections.to: Deep-sea mining exploration
connections.rationale: National and EU targets for critical mineral supply security drive exploration for polymetallic nodules and cobalt-rich crusts on Macaronesian seamounts.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
ISA (2019) "Environmental Management Plan for the Clarion-Clipperton Zone"

connections.to: Deep-sea mining exploration
connections.rationale: Growing demand for undersea communications cable networks to connect Macaronesian islands increases seabed survey activity that can overlap with seamount exploration zones.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Potential disturbance from deep-sea mining
connections.rationale: Exploratory deep-sea mining operations on Macaronesian seamounts generate sediment plumes and physical disturbance, threatening endemic cold-water coral and sponge communities.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
ISA (2019) "Environmental Management Plan for the Clarion-Clipperton Zone"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Deep-sea benthic invertebrate diversity
connections.rationale: Physical seabed disturbance from deep-sea mining exploration poses a severe threat to the slow-recovering deep-sea benthic invertebrate diversity of Macaronesian seamounts, including cold-water corals and sponge aggregations.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
ISA (2019) "Environmental Management Plan for the Clarion-Clipperton Zone"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Potential disturbance from deep-sea mining
connections.rationale: International Seabed Authority regulations set environmental standards for deep-sea mining operations that mitigate but do not eliminate disturbance risks to Macaronesian seamount ecosystems.
references: ISA (2019) "Environmental Management Plan for the Clarion-Clipperton Zone"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

macaronesia_open_coast.description: Open coast and pelagic ecosystems of the Macaronesian region, encompassing the productive waters between volcanic archipelagos where the Canary Current, Azores Current, and North Atlantic subtropical gyre create dynamic oceanographic conditions supporting major tuna fisheries, cetacean migration corridors, and pelagic Sargassum communities.

Climate change and shifting ocean currents
EU Common Fisheries Policy for pelagic stocks

activities.name: Industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing
Pelagic longline fishing for swordfish and sharks
Offshore wind and wave energy development
Pelagic whale and dolphin watching excursions

pressures.name: Overfishing of pelagic and highly migratory stocks
Bycatch of sea turtles and seabirds in longlines
Ocean warming and current regime shifts
Pelagic marine litter and microplastic pollution
Ship strike risk to large cetaceans
Underwater noise from shipping and sonar
Shark finning and targeted shark mortality

states.name: Atlantic tuna stock abundance (bluefin, skipjack, bigeye)
Large cetacean population health (sperm whale, beaked whales)
Pelagic shark and ray population status
Loggerhead and leatherback sea turtle abundance
Cory's shearwater and other seabird colony size

Loss of apex predator trophic function
Reduced marine wildlife encounter rates for ecotourism
Disrupted pelagic food web connectivity
Decline in seabird breeding success

Marine ecotourism income from pelagic wildlife
Regional food security from pelagic fish protein
Cultural significance of traditional tuna fishing
Aesthetic and spiritual value of ocean wilderness

EU shark finning ban and catch limits
IMO shipping lane adjustments for cetacean protection
Seabird bycatch mitigation requirements
Pelagic MPA and OECM designations
OSPAR marine litter regional action plan
Marine spatial planning for offshore activities
Cetacean critical habitat identification programmes

connections.from: Global tuna and pelagic fish market demand
connections.to: Industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing
connections.rationale: International demand for canned and fresh tuna drives the major purse-seine and pole-and-line fleets operating in Macaronesian waters, particularly from the Azores and Canary Islands where tuna fishing is a cornerstone industry.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Global tuna and pelagic fish market demand
connections.to: Pelagic longline fishing for swordfish and sharks
connections.rationale: High market value of swordfish and blue shark drives longline operations across Macaronesian open waters, with the Azores serving as a major base for Portuguese and Spanish pelagic longline fleets.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands

connections.from: Climate change and shifting ocean currents
connections.to: Ocean warming and current regime shifts
connections.rationale: Climate change is altering the strength and position of the Canary Current and Azores Current systems, with warming shifting the subtropical-temperate boundary northward and affecting upwelling patterns that sustain Macaronesian pelagic productivity.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Commercial shipping and vessel transit
connections.rationale: Growing global trade volumes increase shipping traffic through Macaronesian waters, which lie on major transatlantic shipping routes between Europe, Africa, and the Americas.
references: Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems
Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region

connections.to: Pelagic whale and dolphin watching excursions
connections.rationale: Growing ecotourism demand drives expansion of open-ocean whale watching from the Azores, where sperm whales and over 20 cetacean species attract thousands of visitors annually to pelagic excursions.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.from: Industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing
connections.to: Overfishing of pelagic and highly migratory stocks
connections.rationale: Industrial tuna fishing in Macaronesian waters contributes to cumulative fishing mortality on shared Atlantic tuna stocks, with skipjack and bigeye tuna showing signs of overexploitation from combined fleet pressure.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Pelagic longline fishing for swordfish and sharks
connections.to: Bycatch of sea turtles and seabirds in longlines
connections.rationale: Pelagic longlines set in Macaronesian waters incidentally catch loggerhead sea turtles and Cory's shearwaters, with the Azores and Canary Islands longline fisheries identified as significant bycatch hotspots for both species.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Pelagic longline fishing for swordfish and sharks
connections.to: Shark finning and targeted shark mortality
connections.rationale: Longline fleets operating in Macaronesian waters target blue sharks and shortfin mako sharks alongside swordfish, contributing to severe population declines of pelagic shark species in the NE Atlantic.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Ship strike risk to large cetaceans
connections.rationale: Shipping traffic through Macaronesian waters poses lethal ship strike risk to sperm whales, fin whales, and beaked whales, with the Canary Islands documented as a high-risk area for ship-whale collisions.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Pelagic marine litter and microplastic pollution
connections.rationale: Commercial vessels transiting Macaronesian waters contribute to pelagic marine litter through operational discharges and cargo loss, adding to oceanic debris accumulation in the subtropical convergence zone.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Underwater noise from shipping and sonar
connections.rationale: Chronic low-frequency noise from shipping in Macaronesian waters masks cetacean communication signals, particularly affecting deep-diving species like sperm whales and beaked whales in the acoustic-sensitive deep channels between islands.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems

connections.from: Overfishing of pelagic and highly migratory stocks
connections.to: Atlantic tuna stock abundance (bluefin, skipjack, bigeye)
connections.rationale: Cumulative fishing pressure from multiple fleets reduces tuna stock abundance in Macaronesian waters, with ICCAT assessments showing several Atlantic tuna stocks at or below maximum sustainable yield.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Shark finning and targeted shark mortality
connections.to: Pelagic shark and ray population status
connections.rationale: Intensive shark fishing in Macaronesian waters has driven severe population declines in blue sharks, shortfin makos, and hammerheads, with the NE Atlantic identified as a global hotspot for shark overexploitation.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Bycatch of sea turtles and seabirds in longlines
connections.to: Loggerhead and leatherback sea turtle abundance
connections.rationale: Longline bycatch mortality in Macaronesian waters significantly affects loggerhead sea turtle populations, as juvenile loggerheads from western Atlantic nesting beaches concentrate in the Azores-Canaries region during their oceanic developmental phase.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Bycatch of sea turtles and seabirds in longlines
connections.to: Cory's shearwater and other seabird colony size
connections.rationale: Longline bycatch kills adult Cory's shearwaters during the breeding season in the Azores and Canary Islands, where Macaronesia hosts the world's largest breeding population of this species.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region

connections.from: Ship strike risk to large cetaceans
connections.to: Large cetacean population health (sperm whale, beaked whales)
connections.rationale: Ship strikes are a documented cause of mortality for sperm whales and fin whales in the Canary Islands, where high shipping traffic intersects with critical cetacean habitat and migration routes.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Ocean warming and current regime shifts
connections.to: Atlantic tuna stock abundance (bluefin, skipjack, bigeye)
connections.rationale: Warming waters in the Macaronesian region are shifting tuna distribution patterns, with tropical species expanding northward and temperate species retreating, altering traditional fishing grounds around the Azores and Canaries.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Pelagic marine litter and microplastic pollution
connections.to: Loggerhead and leatherback sea turtle abundance
connections.rationale: Juvenile loggerhead sea turtles in Macaronesian waters ingest floating plastic debris mistaken for jellyfish prey, with studies documenting microplastic contamination in a high proportion of stranded turtles across the Canary Islands.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Atlantic tuna stock abundance (bluefin, skipjack, bigeye)
connections.to: Declining pelagic fishery catch rates
connections.rationale: Reduced tuna stock abundance translates directly to declining catch rates for Macaronesian tuna fleets, threatening the viability of pole-and-line and purse-seine operations based in the Azores and Canary Islands.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Pelagic shark and ray population status
connections.to: Loss of apex predator trophic function
connections.rationale: Decline of pelagic sharks in Macaronesian waters removes top-down control on mesopredator populations, with documented trophic cascades affecting prey community structure in the open NE Atlantic.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Large cetacean population health (sperm whale, beaked whales)
connections.to: Reduced marine wildlife encounter rates for ecotourism
connections.rationale: Declining cetacean populations reduce encounter rates for whale-watching operations in the Azores and Canary Islands, directly affecting the reliability and quality of the ecotourism product.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.from: Cory's shearwater and other seabird colony size
connections.to: Decline in seabird breeding success
connections.rationale: Reduced colony sizes of Cory's shearwaters and other seabirds on Macaronesian islands diminish breeding success through Allee effects and reduced social facilitation at colonies in the Azores, Madeira, and Canary Islands.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Tuya et al. (2014) Biogeography of the Macaronesian region

connections.to: Tuna fishing industry revenue and employment
connections.rationale: Declining tuna catch rates directly reduce revenue and threaten employment in the Macaronesian tuna fishing industry, which is a major employer in Azores and Canary Islands coastal communities.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Reduced marine wildlife encounter rates for ecotourism
connections.to: Marine ecotourism income from pelagic wildlife
connections.rationale: Lower cetacean encounter rates reduce tourist satisfaction and willingness to pay for whale-watching excursions in the Azores and Canary Islands, threatening the viability of marine ecotourism businesses.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.to: Regional food security from pelagic fish protein
connections.rationale: Reduced local tuna and pelagic fish landings affect food security on Macaronesian islands where fresh fish is a dietary staple, increasing dependence on imported frozen seafood.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Tuna fishing industry revenue and employment
connections.to: ICCAT tuna stock management measures
connections.rationale: Economic pressure from declining Macaronesian tuna fisheries contributes to political support for ICCAT management measures, though industry also resists strict quota reductions that threaten short-term livelihoods.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Marine ecotourism income from pelagic wildlife
connections.to: Cetacean critical habitat identification programmes
connections.rationale: The economic value of whale watching in the Azores and Canary Islands motivates investment in cetacean research and critical habitat mapping to support conservation of the resource base.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas

connections.to: Overfishing of pelagic and highly migratory stocks
connections.rationale: ICCAT quota allocations and fishing effort controls for Atlantic tuna stocks reduce cumulative fishing mortality in Macaronesian waters, with the successful recovery of Atlantic bluefin tuna demonstrating management effectiveness.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Brito et al. (2011) Fishes of the Canary Islands
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: EU shark finning ban and catch limits
connections.to: Shark finning and targeted shark mortality
connections.rationale: The EU fins-attached regulation and emerging catch limits for blue and mako sharks reduce targeted shark mortality by Macaronesian-based longline fleets, though enforcement at sea remains challenging.
references: Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: IMO shipping lane adjustments for cetacean protection
connections.to: Ship strike risk to large cetaceans
connections.rationale: IMO-endorsed traffic separation scheme adjustments in the Canary Islands have reduced ship strike risk by rerouting vessels away from known sperm whale and fin whale concentration areas.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Bycatch of sea turtles and seabirds in longlines
connections.rationale: Mandatory use of bird-scaring lines, weighted hooks, and night setting by longline vessels in Macaronesian waters reduces incidental capture of Cory's shearwaters and other seabirds during fishing operations.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.to: Overfishing of pelagic and highly migratory stocks
connections.rationale: Designation of pelagic MPAs and other effective conservation measures in Macaronesian waters provides spatial protection for critical tuna spawning and feeding areas, complementing stock-wide management measures.
references: Afonso et al. (2020) Azores marine protected areas
Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Spalding et al. (2007) Marine Ecoregions of the World

connections.from: OSPAR marine litter regional action plan
connections.to: Pelagic marine litter and microplastic pollution
connections.rationale: OSPAR's Regional Action Plan on Marine Litter addresses land-based and sea-based sources of litter entering Macaronesian waters, though oceanic transport from distant sources limits the effectiveness of regional measures alone.
references: Santos et al. (2020) Marine litter in the Macaronesian region
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Underwater noise from shipping and sonar
connections.to: Large cetacean population health (sperm whale, beaked whales)
connections.rationale: Chronic anthropogenic noise in Macaronesian deep channels affects beaked whales and sperm whales, with mass strandings of beaked whales in the Canary Islands linked to military sonar exercises in adjacent waters.
references: Monteiro et al. (2008) Cetacean diversity in the Azores
Hernandez et al. (2018) Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: Loss of apex predator trophic function
connections.to: Disrupted pelagic food web connectivity
connections.rationale: Removal of sharks and large tuna from Macaronesian pelagic ecosystems triggers mesopredator release and trophic cascades that propagate through the open ocean food web, reducing overall ecosystem stability.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) Marine biodiversity in the Azores
Lloret et al. (2008) Fishing as a driver of change in the Macaronesian marine environment
Halpern et al. (2008) A global map of human impact on marine ecosystems

connections.from: EU Common Fisheries Policy for pelagic stocks
connections.to: Industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing
connections.rationale: EU Common Fisheries Policy sets total allowable catches and technical measures for pelagic stocks in Macaronesian waters, directly regulating industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing operations.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
EU (2013) "Regulation (EU) No 1380/2013 on the Common Fisheries Policy"

connections.to: Offshore wind and wave energy development
connections.rationale: National and EU offshore renewable energy targets are catalysing offshore wind and wave energy projects in exposed Macaronesian open-coast waters with high wind and wave energy resources.
references: Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.from: Offshore wind and wave energy development
connections.to: Pelagic shark and ray population status
connections.rationale: Construction noise and electromagnetic fields from offshore energy infrastructure can disturb pelagic elasmobranchs and alter the movement patterns of pelagic sharks and rays in Macaronesian open-coast waters.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Pelagic Sargassum community extent
connections.rationale: Scientific expeditions monitor Sargassum drift dynamics around Macaronesian open coasts, generating data that guides management responses to increasing Sargassum strandings.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"

connections.to: Large cetacean population health (sperm whale, beaked whales)
connections.rationale: Military sonar exercises in Macaronesian waters are linked to cetacean strandings, as the Canary Islands and Azores host major naval exercise zones that overlap with sperm whale and beaked whale migration corridors.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Marine ecotourism income from pelagic wildlife
connections.rationale: Increasing Sargassum strandings on Macaronesian beaches reduce beach attractiveness and marine ecotourism income from pelagic wildlife encounters, affecting island economies dependent on marine recreation.
references: Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"

connections.from: Industrial tuna purse-seine and pole-and-line fishing
connections.to: Cultural significance of traditional tuna fishing
connections.rationale: The continuation of traditional and industrial tuna fishing practices in Macaronesian communities directly sustains the cultural significance of tuna fishing as a key element of local cultural identity and intergenerational knowledge transfer.
references: Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"

connections.to: Aesthetic and spiritual value of ocean wilderness
connections.rationale: Growing marine ecotourism demand centred on pelagic wildlife in Macaronesian waters reflects and reinforces the aesthetic and spiritual value that island residents and visitors place on the region's ocean wilderness.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Hernández et al. (2018) "Tourism impacts on Canary Islands marine ecosystems"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"

connections.from: Marine spatial planning for offshore activities
connections.to: Offshore wind and wave energy development
connections.rationale: Marine spatial planning frameworks in Macaronesian EEZs designate zones for offshore energy development, reducing spatial conflicts and providing regulatory certainty for developers.
references: Tuya et al. (2014) "Biogeography of the Macaronesian region"
Friedlander et al. (2017) "Coral reef ecosystems of the Macaronesian Atlantic"

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Nursery function for offshore shrimp and fish stocks
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Reef fish species diversity and biomass
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Over 80% of commercial reef fish species use mangrove habitats as nurseries during juvenile stages before migrating to reef systems

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Nursery function for offshore shrimp and fish stocks
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Reef fish biomass and trophic structure
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Indo-Pacific mangroves serve as critical nursery habitat for juvenile reef fish, with mangrove presence increasing adjacent reef fish biomass by up to 25%

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Nursery habitat provision for conch and lobster
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Reef fish species diversity and biomass
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Caribbean Thalassia seagrass beds provide essential nursery habitat for juvenile grunts, snappers and parrotfish that recruit to adjacent coral reefs as sub-adults

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Mangrove canopy cover and forest extent
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Sedimentation from dredging and land clearing
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Mangrove root systems trap terrestrial sediments; mangrove loss releases sediment plumes that smother adjacent seagrass beds and reduce light availability

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Coastal wave protection by reef barrier
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Mangrove canopy cover and forest extent
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Healthy reef barriers dissipate 70-97% of wave energy; reef degradation exposes coastal mangroves to increased wave erosion and storm damage

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Nursery habitat for marine and diadromous species
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Atlantic bluefin tuna stock recovery status
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Estuaries export juvenile fish that contribute to offshore forage fish stocks, indirectly supporting pelagic predator biomass through food web linkages

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Nutrient filtration by estuarine wetlands
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Estuarine wetlands filter 20-80% of nitrogen loads; reduced filtration capacity increases nutrient export to offshore waters, fuelling deep-water hypoxia

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Ocean carbon sequestration (biological pump)
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Coastal water clarity and photic zone depth
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Offshore upwelling brings nutrient-rich deep water to the coastal zone, driving phytoplankton blooms that reduce water clarity and alter coastal ecosystem dynamics

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Fish provisioning from lagoon fisheries
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Baltic lagoons function as nurseries for pike-perch and perch that migrate to coastal waters as adults, contributing to nearshore fish community biomass

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Coastal water quality (bathing water standards)
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Lagoon water quality depends on tidal exchange with coastal waters; polluted coastal waters import contaminants and nutrients during tidal flushing, degrading lagoon oxygen conditions

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Coastal water clarity and photic zone depth
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Intertidal and subtidal volcanic reef community composition
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Mainland-origin nutrient plumes and sediment loads can reach island waters via ocean currents, reducing water clarity and affecting reef community composition

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Beach recreational and surf tourism amenity
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Growing mainland coastal tourism demand drives overflow to island destinations, increasing visitor pressure on whale-watching and marine ecotourism beyond sustainable capacity

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Water clarity maintenance through sediment trapping
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Seagrass meadows trap and stabilise sediments, maintaining the clear water conditions essential for coral photosynthesis and reef health

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Eastern Baltic cod stock biomass
cross_ecosystem_links.to_element: Nursery function for coastal fish species
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Offshore cod stock status affects predation pressure on coastal forage fish; cod stock collapse alters estuarine fish community structure through reduced top-down control

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Posidonia oceanica meadow extent and density
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Coastal Posidonia meadows and lagoon seagrasses form connected habitat networks; loss of coastal meadows reduces propagule supply and genetic connectivity to lagoon populations

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Benthic invertebrate biomass (cockle, mussel, lugworm)
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Tidal flat invertebrate productivity supports juvenile fish nursery function for North Sea offshore stocks.

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Estuarine fish community (smelt, flounder, bass)
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Estuaries serve as critical nursery habitats exporting juvenile fish to North Sea offshore populations.

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Deep-water renewal frequency and oxygen levels
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Fjord deep-water renewal is driven by dense water formation linked to sea ice dynamics.

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Kelp forest (Laminaria hyperborea/digitata) canopy cover
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Kelp-derived particulate organic carbon contributes to offshore biological carbon pump through detrital export.

cross_ecosystem_links.from_element: Atoll reef crest structural integrity
cross_ecosystem_links.rationale: Global coral reef health is interconnected through ocean-scale larval dispersal and shared climate stressors.
